_comment: AWS AIP-C01 HARD question bank. Official practice-exam style: rich scenarios, LEAST/MOST-overhead framing, Select-TWO items, and strong distractors with per-option explanations. To add questions append objects to 'questions'. Each: {id, task (key in 'tasks'), type ('single'=one correct, 'multi'=all-or-nothing), question, options:[{text, correct(bool), explanation}]}. 'multi' needs >=2 correct:true. Optional 'source' field (e.g. 'BenchPrep') shows a badge in the app.
exam: AWS Certified Generative AI Developer – Professional (AIP-C01)

Deploy models for inference (Amazon SageMaker AI)

2.3.name: Dev Tools & Automation (Amazon Q Developer, CI/CD)

questions.question: An education company built a content generation system on Amazon Bedrock that generates practice questions to quiz end users on a topic. The system consumes a mix of curated data and scraped data in the topic domain. Reviewers must approve the generated question-response sets before end users can access them. The company wants to add source lineage so reviewers can verify the credibility of the content. Which combination of steps will meet these requirements with the LEAST operational overhead? (Select TWO.)

options.text: Enable Amazon Bedrock invocation logging and correlate the logs with the data source.
options.explanation: Invocation logging tracks model interactions, but correlating logs to data sources requires manual work or custom automation. It captures usage data without helping reviewers verify content credibility.

options.text: Tag FM outputs with metadata from the data source.
options.explanation: Tagging the generated questions with metadata about the curated/scraped sources gives direct, automated traceability between content and its origins and immediate access to source information for reviewers.

options.text: Register the curated and scraped input datasets with AWS Glue Data Catalog.
options.explanation: The Data Catalog creates a searchable inventory of data sources reviewers can use to verify origin and credibility, using a managed service with structured metadata and minimal overhead.

options.text: Use Amazon SageMaker Clarify to explain model predictions.
options.explanation: Clarify provides model explainability and bias detection, not source lineage tracking.

options.text: Use AWS CloudTrail to log reviewer feedback actions.
options.explanation: CloudTrail logs API calls and can record approval/rejection actions, but it does not provide source lineage of the generated content.

questions.question: A company is implementing a systematic evaluation process to replace an in-production FM in Amazon Bedrock with a new model. The change depends on the new model demonstrating better performance, and the company must follow a sequential validation process in which each step is reviewed and approved before proceeding. Which ordering of the five steps is correct?

options.text: Define evaluation metrics (relevance, factual accuracy, fluency) -> Create a test dataset with diverse scenarios and edge cases -> Conduct A/B testing against the production model -> Implement automated quality gates with AWS Step Functions -> Analyze results and generate an evaluation report.
options.explanation: Metrics must be defined first so the dataset and tests can target them; the dataset enables A/B testing; quality gates then enforce the criteria before a final analysis and report.

options.text: Create a test dataset -> Define evaluation metrics -> Conduct A/B testing -> Analyze results and generate a report -> Implement automated quality gates with AWS Step Functions.
options.explanation: Metrics should be defined before the dataset is built, and quality gates belong before the final reporting step, not after.

options.text: Conduct A/B testing -> Define evaluation metrics -> Create a test dataset -> Implement automated quality gates -> Analyze results and generate a report.
options.explanation: You cannot run meaningful A/B testing before metrics and a test dataset exist.

options.text: Define evaluation metrics -> Conduct A/B testing -> Create a test dataset -> Analyze results and generate a report -> Implement automated quality gates.
options.explanation: The test dataset must exist before A/B testing, and gates should precede the final report.

questions.question: A company needs secure authentication for a third-party application that uses Amazon Bedrock. The solution must integrate with the company's existing identity provider (IdP), maintain comprehensive audit logs of authentication and API calls, eliminate long-lived credentials, and provide temporary access to Amazon Bedrock. Which solutions will meet these requirements? (Select TWO.)

options.text: Implement an OpenID Connect (OIDC) integration with Amazon Cognito that authenticates users through the IdP and exchanges tokens for temporary AWS credentials to access Amazon Bedrock.
options.explanation: Cognito with OIDC authenticates through the existing IdP, exchanges identity tokens for temporary AWS credentials (no long-lived keys), and logs via CloudTrail.

options.text: Configure an Amazon API Gateway Lambda authorizer to validate credentials against LDAP and issue signed JWTs for Amazon Bedrock access.
options.explanation: This does not provide temporary AWS credentials, requires custom development, and does not use built-in identity federation.

options.text: Create IAM users for each employee, assign permissions through IAM policies, and rotate credentials with AWS Secrets Manager.
options.explanation: IAM users rely on persistent access keys, do not integrate with the IdP, and do not provide temporary access.

options.text: Create an IAM role and configure federation with AWS STS AssumeRole, storing the application's IAM user credentials in the application configuration.
options.explanation: Storing IAM user credentials violates the requirement to eliminate long-lived credentials and does not integrate with the IdP properly.

options.text: Deploy AWS IAM Identity Center with SAML federation to the IdP and configure custom permission sets that grant access to Amazon Bedrock.
options.explanation: IAM Identity Center with SAML integrates with the IdP, issues temporary credentials (no long-lived keys), and provides audit logging through CloudTrail.

questions.question: A company is developing a RAG application on Amazon Bedrock that processes customer support documents. The application retrieves many relevant documents, but users report that the most relevant information often appears lower in the results. The company wants to improve relevance ranking so the most useful information appears first. Which combination of steps will improve relevance with MINIMAL operational overhead? (Select TWO.)

options.text: Configure Amazon OpenSearch Serverless with the Bedrock Knowledge Bases plugin and use OpenSearch Learning to Rank for relevance scoring.
options.explanation: Learning to Rank is an open-source plugin that requires custom scoring and trained, maintained models — more overhead than built-in Bedrock features.

options.text: Create an Amazon Aurora PostgreSQL database with pgvector and a custom similarity scoring algorithm combining vector distances with metadata.
options.explanation: This requires custom development and managing the vector database — more operational overhead.

options.text: Use Amazon Bedrock reranker models with Amazon OpenSearch Service to reorder retrieved results based on semantic relevance to the query.
options.explanation: Bedrock reranker models are purpose-built to score query-document relevance and reorder results so the most relevant information appears first.

options.text: Use Amazon SageMaker JumpStart FMs with Amazon Kendra Intelligent Ranking to create custom relevancy scoring algorithms.
options.explanation: Bedrock already provides built-in reranking optimized for FM integration; this approach is more complex and less integrated.

options.text: Use Knowledge Bases with hybrid search and Amazon OpenSearch Serverless to combine vector embeddings with keyword matching.
options.explanation: Hybrid search combines semantic vector embeddings with keyword matching to improve retrieval relevance with minimal overhead.

questions.question: A financial services company must deploy existing Python agent code to Amazon Bedrock AgentCore Runtime to reduce infrastructure management and operational complexity. The agent must handle quick data lookups (sub-second) and comprehensive research report generation (streaming responses over several minutes). The solution must automatically manage HTTP server configuration, endpoint routing, and health monitoring. Which deployment approaches will meet these requirements with MINIMAL operational overhead? (Select TWO.)

options.text: Implement a FastAPI server configured with /invocations and /ping endpoints and container orchestration.
options.explanation: FastAPI requires manual configuration of endpoints, JSON/streaming handling, Dockerfiles, and orchestration — more operational overhead.

options.text: Implement the AgentCore SDK with the @app.entrypoint decorator to automatically handle server setup and endpoint management.
options.explanation: The SDK auto-creates an HTTP server on port 8080, implements the required /invocations and /ping endpoints, and supports both JSON and streaming responses without manual configuration.

options.text: Deploy the agent on Amazon ECS on AWS Fargate using a custom container image running the AgentCore SDK.
options.explanation: This requires building and maintaining Dockerfiles, images, and task definitions with no automatic HTTP server setup or health monitoring.

options.text: Deploy the agent on Amazon SageMaker AI real-time endpoints using a custom inference container.
options.explanation: This increases infrastructure overhead: building images, configuring the inference server, auto scaling, and health monitoring.

options.text: Deploy the agent using the AgentCore starter toolkit for automated packaging, containerization, and deployment workflows.
options.explanation: The starter toolkit automates packaging, ARM64 container builds, ECR creation/push, and deployment via CreateAgentRuntime — minimal overhead.

questions.question: A company is building a diagnostic imaging application that performs similarity searches across 50 million images to assist with diagnosis. New images are processed daily, but similarity searches are performed infrequently, only when users need to find similar cases. The company wants a cost-effective solution with responsive search performance and no infrastructure management. Which solution will meet these requirements MOST cost-effectively?

options.text: Store image vectors in Amazon OpenSearch Serverless and use vector search for similarity searches.
options.explanation: OpenSearch Serverless is optimized for high-throughput, frequent searches; you pay for provisioned capacity that stays underutilized with infrequent searches.

options.text: Use Amazon DynamoDB to store image vectors and implement custom similarity search logic with AWS Lambda.
options.explanation: DynamoDB has no built-in vector similarity search; custom Lambda logic adds latency and compute cost.

options.text: Create an Amazon S3 vector bucket with vector indexes to store image embeddings and perform similarity searches.
options.explanation: Amazon S3 Vectors is fully managed and serverless, scales to billions of vectors, suits infrequent searches, requires no provisioning, and bills only for what you use.

options.text: Store image vectors in Amazon RDS for PostgreSQL and use the pgvector extension.
options.explanation: RDS requires provisioning and managing instances (not 'no infrastructure management') and is not cost-effective for infrequent workloads.

questions.question: An ecommerce company uses Amazon Bedrock in a single AWS Region to generate product descriptions and recommendations. During peak periods it receives "Too many requests, please wait before trying again." errors. The company must increase invocation throughput during peaks without additional operational overhead, keep compatibility with the existing Bedrock API, and use the same FM. Which solution will meet these requirements MOST cost-effectively?

options.text: Create an AWS Lambda function to invoke the model in the original Region by default and fall back to a secondary Region.
options.explanation: A Lambda intermediary must be built and maintained, increasing cost and operational overhead versus a built-in feature.

options.text: Use cross-Region inference to distribute traffic across multiple Regions within a geographic area.
options.explanation: Cross-Region inference automatically distributes inference requests across multiple Regions in your geography, increasing throughput with no extra infrastructure and the same API/FM.

options.text: Use prompt routing to distribute traffic across multiple FMs from the same family.
options.explanation: Intelligent prompt routing requires at least two different models from the same family; the requirement is to use the same FM.

options.text: Use provisioned throughput to provision a higher level of throughput for the FM.
options.explanation: Provisioned throughput suits consistent, predictable usage, not occasional peaks, and is less cost-effective here.

questions.question: A company uses an AI assistant that answers questions from internal documents stored in Amazon S3, using Amazon Bedrock Knowledge Bases with S3 as the data source. The company wants new documents reflected in responses as soon as possible and deleted documents excluded as soon as possible. A developer must build a scalable, event-driven, and resilient solution. Which solution will meet these requirements?

options.text: Configure EventBridge Scheduler to run every 5 minutes and invoke a Lambda that tracks S3 changes and calls IngestKnowledgeBaseDocuments / DeleteKnowledgeBaseDocuments.
options.explanation: This is time-based rather than near real-time, and tracking changes in Lambda is not operationally efficient.

options.text: Configure S3 Event Notifications to invoke a Lambda on object-created/deleted events that calls IngestKnowledgeBaseDocuments / DeleteKnowledgeBaseDocuments.
options.explanation: Direct S3-to-Lambda invocation has no buffering or retry support and can fail under high load.

options.text: Configure S3 Event Notifications to send object-created/deleted events to an Amazon SQS queue, and have a Lambda poll the queue and call IngestKnowledgeBaseDocuments / DeleteKnowledgeBaseDocuments.
options.explanation: SQS buffers events between S3 and Lambda, absorbs spikes, provides automatic retries, and ensures no updates are missed — scalable and resilient.

options.text: Configure EventBridge Scheduler to run every 5 minutes and invoke a Lambda that syncs S3 with the knowledge base via StartIngestionJob.
options.explanation: Periodic full scanning is inefficient, introduces delays, and is not event-driven.

questions.question: A financial services company runs a RAG application that answers questions from internal market analysis reports. It uses an Amazon Bedrock embedding model and an Amazon OpenSearch Service cluster as the vector store; a Lambda function performs embedding and search. After a code update, the app returns generic responses such as 'no relevant information found' even for previously answerable questions. CloudWatch Logs shows no errors, X-Ray confirms successful FM invocation, the cluster is healthy, and query latency is normal. What is the cause of this issue?

options.text: The document embeddings in OpenSearch were deleted during the update and not re-indexed.
options.explanation: Deleted embeddings would surface in logs or failed queries; with no errors and normal latency, documents are still being retrieved.

options.text: The Lambda function's IAM role is missing bedrock:InvokeModel permission.
options.explanation: The model is invoked successfully; a missing permission would throw an access error.

options.text: The Amazon Bedrock FM temperature parameter was increased.
options.explanation: Temperature affects generation randomness, not retrieval; this is a retrieval failure.

options.text: The updated Lambda function uses a different version of the embedding model.
options.explanation: Embedding drift: query embeddings generated by a different model than was used to index documents cause a vector-space mismatch and ineffective retrieval.

questions.question: A company built an AI assistant on Amazon Bedrock with multiple guardrails: prompt injection detection, sensitive information filtering, and denied topic blocking. When a query is blocked, a developer needs detailed analysis of which specific guardrail rule was invoked and why content was flagged, in order to fine-tune configurations and distinguish legitimate queries from threats. Which configuration provides the MOST detailed analysis of guardrail decision-making for content filtering?

options.text: Enable Amazon Bedrock model evaluation with automated jobs including guardrail assessment metrics, test prompt injection resistance, and analyze via the evaluation dashboard.
options.explanation: Model evaluation is for measurable, offline tests and reports, not analysis during live inference.

options.text: Enable model invocation logging, configure CloudWatch alarms on InvocationsIntervened filtered by GuardrailContentSource, and analyze with CloudWatch Insights.
options.explanation: Invocation logging captures input/output/metadata but not guardrail intervention details.

options.text: Configure guardrail tracing with {"trace": "enabled"} and monitor InvocationsIntervened filtered by GuardrailContentSource to identify whether input prompts or output responses triggered interventions.
options.explanation: GuardrailContentSource distinguishes input vs. output but not which guardrail policy layer intervened.

options.text: Configure guardrail tracing with {"trace": "enabled"} and monitor InvocationsIntervened filtered by the GuardrailPolicyType dimensions ContentPolicy, TopicPolicy, and SensitiveInformationPolicy.
options.explanation: GuardrailPolicyType identifies which specific policy intervened, enabling informed tuning based on the exact rule triggered.

questions.question: A financial services company wants a mobile app to help users with account inquiries and general account information. It has a large volume of customer-support email exchanges to use as source material, stored in Amazon S3. The data contains personally identifiable information (PII) that must not appear in search results. Which solution will meet these requirements?

options.text: Use Amazon Kendra for enterprise search and integrate with an Amazon Bedrock FM, using a system prompt to identify and remove PII during query processing.
options.explanation: System prompts cannot reliably or consistently remove PII and can be jailbroken — not secure for sensitive financial data.

options.text: Use Amazon Comprehend to detect and redact PII from the email data in S3, and integrate Comprehend with Amazon Kendra to enable enterprise search of the processed data.
options.explanation: Comprehend detects and redacts PII from text, and Kendra provides managed enterprise search over the processed data for conversational integration.

options.text: Use Amazon Textract to extract text, Amazon Macie to scan for PII in S3, and integrate Textract and S3 with Amazon Kendra.
options.explanation: Macie is for data discovery/security posture, not PII redaction for GenAI; Textract targets scanned documents, not raw email text.

options.text: Use Amazon Comprehend to detect and redact PII, and integrate with Amazon DocumentDB for database queries.
options.explanation: DocumentDB requires custom search development and lacks NLP for natural-language interactions; it is not enterprise search.

questions.question: A company wants an application that analyzes videos and photos from public fashion shows to understand style elements and trends, stores the extracted information, and provides a dashboard summarizing trends. Which solution will meet these requirements with the LEAST operational overhead?

options.text: Use Amazon EventBridge to trigger Lambda functions that use Amazon QuickSight Q to analyze the videos and photos, store results in S3, and deploy a QuickSight dashboard with ML-powered trend analysis.
options.explanation: QuickSight Q answers questions about data already in a dashboard; it cannot analyze videos or photos.

options.text: Use AWS Step Functions to process videos and photos with Amazon Bedrock multimodal FMs, store results in S3, and use an Amazon QuickSight dashboard to visualize trends.
options.explanation: Bedrock multimodal FMs (e.g., Amazon Nova Pro, Claude) analyze visual content directly without custom model development; Step Functions orchestrates and QuickSight builds dashboards serverlessly.

options.text: Use Amazon Rekognition Custom Labels to train a custom model, store results in DynamoDB, and create an Amazon Managed Grafana dashboard with custom plugins.
options.explanation: This requires custom model training/maintenance and custom Grafana plugin development — more overhead.

options.text: Use an Anthropic Claude model in Bedrock to analyze text descriptions, use Stable Diffusion for image analysis, store results in OpenSearch Service, and create a Managed Grafana dashboard.
options.explanation: This manages an OpenSearch cluster and multiple FMs with custom visualizations, and analyzes text descriptions rather than the visual content directly.

questions.question: A financial services company uses Amazon Bedrock to build an AI assistant for customer-support reps across multiple business units. A developer must ensure prompt templates are governed through approval workflows, and the company requires comprehensive logging of all model invocations with a 7-year retention period for regulatory compliance. Which combination of steps will meet these requirements with MINIMAL operational overhead? (Select TWO.)

options.text: Use Amazon Bedrock Prompt Management with multi-stage approval workflows and IAM policies that require multi-party authorization.
options.explanation: Prompt Management creates, parameterizes, versions, and approves prompt templates with multi-stage approvals and access controls — built-in governance for multiple business units.

options.text: Store prompt templates in DynamoDB tables with composite keys partitioned by business unit and use IAM item-level permissions for approvals.
options.explanation: This requires custom development for approval workflows and access control — more overhead.

options.text: Set up EventBridge rules to capture invocation events, route to CloudWatch Logs by business unit, export to S3, and enable S3 Object Lock compliance mode for 7 years.
options.explanation: This requires custom configuration and maintenance, adds failure points, and does not provide the comprehensive audit trail needed.

options.text: Enable AWS CloudTrail data events for all Bedrock APIs, deliver to CloudTrail Lake with 7-year retention, tag events with business unit ID, and query with CloudTrail Lake.
options.explanation: CloudTrail captures only API metadata, not the prompt content and model responses needed for regulatory reconstruction.

options.text: Enable Amazon Bedrock model invocation logging to Amazon S3, enable S3 Object Lock compliance mode for 7 years, and create separate prefixes per business unit.
options.explanation: Built-in invocation logging needs minimal setup; Object Lock compliance mode enforces immutable 7-year retention; prefixes segregate business units.

questions.question: A company is implementing AI governance requiring all FM interactions to be secured with guardrails. After configuring Amazon Bedrock guardrails, the company must ensure that all InvokeModel and Converse API calls apply the guardrails. Which solution will enforce guardrail compliance for the API calls in the MOST operationally efficient way?

options.text: Configure IAM policies for InvokeModel/Converse with both the bedrock:GuardrailIdentifier and bedrock:PromptRouterArn condition keys, requiring prompt router validation.
options.explanation: PromptRouterArn filters access by prompt router and is unrelated to guardrail enforcement; it adds complexity without benefit.

options.text: Create a Lambda function that validates and enforces guardrails before proxying requests to Bedrock, used as the exclusive endpoint.
options.explanation: A proxy adds a failure point and bottleneck and requires custom code maintenance — less efficient than built-in capabilities.

options.text: Store guardrail identifiers in Systems Manager Parameter Store and have a Lambda retrieve the identifier before each Bedrock call.
options.explanation: Retrieving the identifier per call adds latency and Lambda maintenance overhead.

options.text: Configure IAM policies for InvokeModel/Converse with the bedrock:GuardrailIdentifier condition key, applied to all IAM roles that access Bedrock FMs.
options.explanation: The GuardrailIdentifier condition key centrally and efficiently enforces guardrail compliance across all relevant API calls.

questions.question: A developer is building a virtual assistant using an Anthropic Claude model on Amazon Bedrock. The application sends user queries and expects conversational responses, and the developer wants the application to stop generating output after a specific phrase is produced. Which solution will meet these requirements?

options.text: Add the trigger phrase 'stop at this phrase' in the user prompt.
options.explanation: This relies on the model following instructions, which it may not do reliably.

options.text: Use the stop sequences parameter in the inference call to specify a trigger phrase.
options.explanation: Stop sequences are a built-in API mechanism that halt generation when a specified phrase is produced, directly controlling output.

options.text: Use the top-k parameter to control the diversity of tokens in the output.
options.explanation: Top-k controls sampling diversity, not stopping at a phrase.

options.text: Use the temperature parameter to control the likelihood of the phrase appearing.
options.explanation: Temperature controls randomness/creativity, not the stopping point.

questions.question: A cross-functional team is developing a generative AI application on AWS. The team needs to optimize developer productivity, enforce consistent integration patterns, automate performance tuning, and accelerate AI testing across multiple business units using Amazon Q Developer while maintaining application quality. Which combination of steps will meet these requirements? (Select TWO.)

options.text: Use Amazon Q Developer to analyze code for security best practices, with mandatory manual security-team approval of all code changes before integration.
options.explanation: Mandatory manual approval of all changes creates a bottleneck and does not accelerate workflows or automate tuning/testing.

options.text: Use Amazon Q Developer to retrospectively analyze and document common integration patterns across business units' code bases.
options.explanation: Reactive retrospective analysis fails to use real-time assistance for generation, refactoring, and testing.

options.text: Configure Amazon Q Developer to automatically generate and refactor integration code, provide targeted API usage guidance, and suggest performance optimizations across the modular code base.
options.explanation: This delivers real-time code generation, refactoring, API recommendations, and performance optimization, increasing productivity and reducing integration errors.

options.text: Incorporate Amazon Q Developer to resolve coding issues during merge requests, reserving most refactoring and optimization for periodic manual review cycles.
options.explanation: Limiting use to merge/review stages and infrequent manual cycles does not optimize the core benefits.

options.text: Integrate Amazon Q Developer automated unit and integration test generation into the team's CI/CD pipelines.
options.explanation: Embedding automated test generation into CI/CD continuously validates components, sustaining velocity and quality with early defect detection.

questions.question: A developer deployed a fine-tuned LLM to an Amazon SageMaker AI endpoint using the default continuous-batching configuration with the Deep Java Library (DJL). The model is served on GPU instances with 8 GPUs each. As demand grows, many instances are needed and the developer wants to avoid costs from overutilization. Logs show the maximum I/O sequence length in real requests is 10x smaller than configured, concurrency per instance is low, and profiling shows the model's weights and activations fit entirely within 4 GPUs. Which combination of steps can improve resource utilization? (Select TWO.)

options.text: Increase the number of SageMaker AI instances and spread requests more evenly.
options.explanation: This does not improve concurrency or reduce the GPU memory footprint; serving-property configuration is the better lever.

options.text: Reduce the model's maximum sequence length to allow a higher rolling batch size for each GPU.
options.explanation: With DJL, reducing the max sequence length frees memory for larger batches, increasing throughput and concurrency per instance.

options.text: Enable speculative decoding to reduce response latency for each request.
options.explanation: Speculative decoding improves latency, not resource utilization.

options.text: Use tensor parallelism with a degree of 4 to deploy two model replicas on each instance.
options.explanation: Because the model fits within 4 GPUs, a tensor-parallel degree of 4 creates two replicas across the 8 GPUs, using the hardware fully.

options.text: Split the model across all 8 GPUs using a tensor parallelism degree of 8 to improve memory efficiency.
options.explanation: A degree of 8 serves only one replica across all GPUs, leaving half the capacity underutilized since the model fits in 4 GPUs.

questions.question: A developer tested a pre-trained Hugging Face text-to-image model with Amazon SageMaker JumpStart and now needs to deploy it for on-demand image generation. The solution must use GPUs for inference, handle text datasets up to 50 MB of image descriptions, and provide responses within 15 minutes. Which deployment strategy will meet these requirements?

options.text: Deploy a SageMaker Asynchronous Inference endpoint using an accelerated computing instance type and create a Lambda for on-demand invocation.
options.explanation: Asynchronous inference supports long-running requests (up to ~15 minutes), GPU instances, and large payloads (up to 1 GB), and scales with usage.

options.text: Deploy a SageMaker Serverless Inference endpoint using a general purpose instance type and create a Lambda for invocation.
options.explanation: Serverless inference does not support the GPU instances needed for efficient image generation.

options.text: Deploy a SageMaker Real-Time Inference endpoint using an accelerated computing instance type and create a Lambda for invocation.
options.explanation: Real-time endpoints cap payloads near 25 MB, which does not meet the 50 MB requirement.

options.text: Create a SageMaker AI batch transform job using an accelerated computing instance type and create a Lambda to start the job.
options.explanation: Batch transform is for offline batch processing, not on-demand individual requests within 15 minutes.

questions.question: A news media company wants a content-conformance tool that reviews and adjusts articles against a style guide. Journalists need a web-based editor that provides real-time analysis on request: clicking an 'analyze' button should immediately begin streaming suggested revisions into the editor. Articles carry content categories in metadata (news, sports, editorial). The company wants an Amazon Bedrock FM to analyze content and provide immediate feedback. Which architecture will meet these requirements with the LEAST operational overhead?

options.text: Use an SQS queue for article ingestion, Step Functions to process content, a Lambda to determine category and invoke Bedrock with style-guide prompts, DynamoDB for results, and an API Gateway WebSocket API for streaming.
options.explanation: SQS requires polling and introduces latency, so it does not provide immediate feedback when journalists click 'analyze'.

options.text: Deploy an API Gateway WebSocket API linked to a Lambda that reads the content category from metadata, routes to the appropriate Bedrock model, uses Bedrock Prompt Management for style-guide rules, and uses the Bedrock streaming API to return suggestions in real time.
options.explanation: WebSocket APIs provide real-time bidirectional communication; the Lambda routes by metadata, Prompt Management manages rules, and the streaming API returns suggestions immediately with minimal infrastructure.

options.text: Create an API Gateway REST API with Lambda function URLs for response streaming using chunked transfer encoding.
options.explanation: REST APIs are not suited to long-lived real-time streaming connections; size limits and timeouts produce a poor experience.

options.text: Configure an Application Load Balancer with Amazon ECS tasks running custom containers that implement routing and style checking using Bedrock streaming and WebSocket connections.
options.explanation: Running custom containers on ECS requires managing infrastructure, scaling, and deployment — more effort than serverless.

questions.question: A company runs a question-answering application that uses an Amazon Bedrock knowledge base ingesting documents from multiple Amazon S3 buckets. The company needs to monitor the data ingestion process to identify and troubleshoot document-processing issues. Which solution will meet these requirements?

options.text: Configure knowledge base logging with Amazon CloudWatch Logs as the destination and use CloudWatch Logs Insights to query for failed document processing.
options.explanation: Knowledge bases support built-in logging to CloudWatch Logs that records per-file ingestion status (ingested, ignored, failed); Logs Insights can query statuses such as RESOURCE_IGNORED, EMBEDDING_FAILED, or INDEXING_FAILED.

options.text: Enable Amazon CloudWatch Application Signals to automatically detect and alert on knowledge base performance issues.
options.explanation: Application Signals monitors application performance/health and does not integrate with knowledge base ingestion monitoring.

options.text: Enable AWS CloudTrail to track all API calls related to knowledge base operations and document ingestion.
options.explanation: CloudTrail tracks API calls but not the document-level processing information needed to troubleshoot ingestion.

options.text: Implement Amazon Bedrock model invocation logging to capture metrics about document processing and embedding generation.
options.explanation: Model invocation logging captures inference requests, not knowledge base ingestion processes.

questions.question: A company runs a high-volume Amazon Bedrock summarization workload with steady, predictable traffic 24 hours a day. The team wants the lowest cost per token while guaranteeing that capacity is always available during business-critical hours. Following the Cost Optimization pillar of the Generative AI Lens, which approach is MOST appropriate?

options.text: Purchase Provisioned Throughput with a committed term for the model.
options.explanation: For steady, high, predictable volume, a committed Provisioned Throughput model unit lowers cost per token and guarantees dedicated capacity.

options.text: Use on-demand inference for every request.
options.explanation: On-demand pricing suits spiky or unpredictable low volume; at steady high volume it is not the lowest cost and does not reserve capacity.

options.text: Run the workload as a nightly Bedrock batch inference job.
options.explanation: Batch inference is for offline, latency-tolerant jobs; it cannot serve steady real-time traffic.

options.text: Increase max tokens per request so fewer calls are needed.
options.explanation: Larger outputs raise cost per call and do not reduce the number of distinct summaries needed.

questions.question: A regulated company is applying the security and responsible-AI guidance of the Generative AI Lens to a Bedrock assistant. It needs a managed, configurable control plane to block denied topics, filter harmful content, and redact PII consistently across multiple foundation models. Which AWS capability best meets this need with the LEAST custom code?

options.text: Amazon Bedrock Guardrails.
options.explanation: Guardrails provide configurable, model-agnostic policies for denied topics, content filters, word filters, and PII redaction that apply consistently across FMs.

options.text: A set of IAM identity-based policies on the model.
options.explanation: IAM controls who can call the model, not what content the model may produce or must redact.

options.text: CloudWatch alarms on invocation metrics.
options.explanation: Alarms detect operational anomalies after the fact; they do not filter or redact content.

options.text: Lowering the model temperature parameter.
options.explanation: Temperature affects randomness, not topic blocking, content filtering, or PII redaction.

questions.question: An interactive customer assistant must minimize the latency end users perceive while keeping output quality acceptable for routine questions. Which combination best reflects the Performance Efficiency pillar for a Bedrock workload?

options.text: Select a smaller, latency-optimized (or distilled) model and stream tokens to the client.
options.explanation: Right-sizing to a smaller/distilled model reduces inference time, and response streaming lowers perceived latency by showing tokens as they are generated.

options.text: Always use the largest available model for every request.
options.explanation: The largest model maximizes capability but adds latency and cost that routine questions do not require.

options.text: Route all interactive traffic through batch inference.
options.explanation: Batch inference is asynchronous and offline; it is unsuitable for interactive latency.

options.text: Increase max tokens to ensure complete answers.
options.explanation: Larger outputs increase generation time, worsening perceived latency.

questions.question: A platform team wants to operationalize quality assurance for a deployed generative AI feature so regressions are caught automatically as models and prompts change. Which practice best reflects the Operational Excellence pillar?

options.text: Establish automated evaluation pipelines with continuous monitoring of output quality and drift, feeding results back into release decisions.
options.explanation: Operational Excellence emphasizes automating evaluation and observability so quality is measured continuously and improvements are driven by data.

options.text: Run a single manual quality check before the initial launch.
options.explanation: A one-time check does not catch regressions introduced by later model or prompt changes.

options.text: Rely on end-user complaints to surface quality problems.
options.explanation: Waiting for complaints is reactive and lets defects reach users undetected.

options.text: Monitor only infrastructure metrics such as CPU and memory.
options.explanation: Infrastructure health says nothing about generation quality or drift.

questions.question: A security team is hardening a Bedrock workload according to the Security pillar of the Generative AI Lens. Which TWO practices should they adopt? (Select TWO.)

options.text: Apply least-privilege IAM with condition keys (for example, requiring a specific bedrock:GuardrailIdentifier) for model access.
options.explanation: Least-privilege identity-based policies with condition keys restrict who can call models and can enforce that guardrails are applied.

options.text: Encrypt data at rest with AWS KMS and reach Bedrock through VPC endpoints to keep traffic on the AWS network.
options.explanation: Customer-managed encryption and private connectivity protect data confidentiality and reduce exposure of prompts and outputs.

options.text: Embed long-lived IAM access keys in the application package.
options.explanation: Long-lived embedded keys are a major security risk and violate credential best practices.

options.text: Send prompts to a public endpoint without TLS to reduce overhead.
options.explanation: Unencrypted transport exposes sensitive prompt and response data.

options.text: Disable all logging to minimize the data footprint.
options.explanation: Disabling logging removes the audit trail needed for security monitoring and compliance.

questions.question: A company must classify tens of millions of archived documents with a foundation model. Results are consumed by an analytics team the next day, so real-time latency is unnecessary. Which approach best satisfies the Cost Optimization and Sustainability pillars?

options.text: Use Amazon Bedrock batch inference with a right-sized model.
options.explanation: Batch inference processes large datasets asynchronously at lower cost, and a right-sized model avoids over-provisioning compute for a latency-tolerant job.

options.text: Provision dedicated throughput and keep it running 24/7.
options.explanation: A continuously provisioned endpoint wastes capacity for a once-daily, latency-tolerant job.

options.text: Invoke the model in real time, one document per request, as fast as possible.
options.explanation: High-volume real-time invocation is more expensive and unnecessary when results are needed only the next day.

options.text: Use the largest available model for every document in real time.
options.explanation: This maximizes cost and energy use with no benefit for a non-interactive workload.

questions.question: A RAG chatbot resends a large, static system prompt plus a lengthy policy document with every request. Token costs and latency are climbing because the same context is reprocessed each time. Which Bedrock capability reduces cost and latency for the repeated context with the LEAST change to the application?

options.text: Enable prompt caching so the static portion of the prompt is reused across requests.
options.explanation: Prompt caching stores and reuses repeated prompt prefixes, cutting both the tokens billed and the latency for the unchanging context.

options.text: Increase the temperature parameter.
options.explanation: Temperature changes output randomness and has no effect on cost or latency of repeated context.

options.text: Switch the workload to Provisioned Throughput.
options.explanation: Provisioned Throughput reserves capacity but still reprocesses the repeated context on every call and changes the pricing model significantly.

options.text: Move to a model with a larger context window.
options.explanation: A larger context window does not reduce the cost of reprocessing the same tokens each request.

questions.question: A company needs a high-quality, human-labeled dataset to support supervised fine-tuning of a model. It wants managed labeling workflows, the option of a private or vendor workforce, and built-in quality controls, with minimal infrastructure to manage. Which service is MOST appropriate?

options.text: Amazon SageMaker Ground Truth.
options.explanation: Ground Truth provides managed data-labeling workflows, workforce options (private, vendor, or Mechanical Turk), and quality features such as annotation consolidation.

options.text: AWS Glue DataBrew.
options.explanation: DataBrew cleans and normalizes tabular data; it does not run human annotation workflows.

options.text: Amazon Comprehend.
options.explanation: Comprehend performs automated NLP such as entity and PII detection, not managed human labeling.

options.text: Amazon Macie.
options.explanation: Macie discovers and classifies sensitive data; it does not label datasets for training.

questions.question: A data scientist must clean, transform, and engineer features from large tabular datasets before training, and wants a mostly visual, low-code flow that exports prepared data to Amazon S3. Which service fits best?

options.text: Amazon SageMaker Data Wrangler.
options.explanation: Data Wrangler offers a visual, low-code interface for importing, cleaning, transforming, and exporting tabular data for ML, reducing custom preprocessing code.

options.text: Amazon Athena.
options.explanation: Athena queries data in S3 with SQL but is not a guided data-preparation and feature-engineering tool.

options.text: Amazon QuickSight.
options.explanation: QuickSight is for business-intelligence dashboards, not ML data preparation.

options.text: Amazon Kendra.
options.explanation: Kendra is an enterprise search service, not a data-prep tool.

questions.question: Thousands of raw JSON and CSV documents land in Amazon S3 daily and must be cleaned, normalized, and partitioned at scale before being ingested into a knowledge base. The team wants a serverless solution with the LEAST operational overhead. Which service should they use?

options.text: AWS Glue serverless ETL jobs.
options.explanation: Glue runs serverless Apache Spark ETL that cleans, transforms, and partitions large datasets without provisioning or managing clusters.

options.text: An Amazon EMR cluster running Spark.
options.explanation: EMR is powerful but requires provisioning and managing cluster capacity, adding operational overhead.

options.text: A fleet of EC2 instances running batch scripts.
options.explanation: Self-managed EC2 batch processing maximizes operational overhead.

options.text: One AWS Lambda invocation per file with custom parsing.
options.explanation: Per-file Lambda parsing is hard to coordinate at scale and hits timeout and orchestration limits for large ETL.

questions.question: Before building a training corpus, a company must discover and classify where sensitive data and PII reside across hundreds of Amazon S3 buckets. Which service is purpose-built for this discovery task?

options.text: Amazon Macie.
options.explanation: Macie uses managed data identifiers and machine learning to discover and classify sensitive data and PII across S3 at scale.

options.text: Amazon Comprehend.
options.explanation: Comprehend detects and can redact PII within individual text inputs but is not designed to inventory sensitive data across many buckets.

options.text: Amazon Inspector.
options.explanation: Inspector assesses workloads for software vulnerabilities, not sensitive-data classification.

options.text: Amazon GuardDuty.
options.explanation: GuardDuty is a threat-detection service for accounts and workloads, not data classification.

questions.question: A company plans to fine-tune a model on customer chat transcripts but must programmatically remove personally identifiable information from the free text before training. Which service should it use to detect and redact the PII?

options.text: Amazon Comprehend PII detection and redaction.
options.explanation: Comprehend can detect PII entities in text and redact or mask them, producing sanitized transcripts suitable for training.

options.text: Amazon Macie.
options.explanation: Macie classifies and reports on sensitive data in S3 but does not redact PII inside text for downstream training.

options.text: Amazon Textract.
options.explanation: Textract extracts text from scanned documents; it does not specialize in PII redaction of conversational text.

options.text: Amazon Rekognition.
options.explanation: Rekognition analyzes images and video, not text PII.

questions.question: Before fine-tuning, a team wants to measure class imbalance and representation bias in the training dataset so they can correct it. Which capability is designed for pre-training bias analysis?

options.text: Amazon SageMaker Clarify.
options.explanation: Clarify computes pre-training bias metrics on datasets (and post-training bias and feature-attribution explanations on models).

options.text: Amazon SageMaker Model Monitor.
options.explanation: Model Monitor detects drift on a deployed endpoint, not bias in a training dataset before training.

options.text: Amazon CloudWatch.
options.explanation: CloudWatch monitors operational metrics, not dataset bias.

options.text: Amazon Comprehend.
options.explanation: Comprehend performs NLP tasks, not dataset bias measurement.

questions.question: A developer is assembling a dataset to fine-tune an Amazon Bedrock model for a supervised task. What is the correct way to provide the training data?

options.text: Upload a JSONL file of prompt/completion (or messages) examples to Amazon S3 in the format the model expects.
options.explanation: Bedrock customization expects labeled examples as JSONL records in S3 that match the target model's schema.

options.text: Upload a single large PDF of reference material.
options.explanation: An unstructured PDF is not a labeled training dataset; that pattern suits RAG ingestion, not supervised fine-tuning.

options.text: Provide only unlabeled raw text with no prompt/response pairs.
options.explanation: Unlabeled text is for continued pre-training, not supervised fine-tuning, which needs labeled pairs.

options.text: Point the job at a DynamoDB table of records.
options.explanation: Fine-tuning reads training data from S3 in the required file format, not from DynamoDB.

questions.question: A governance team must document each model's intended use, training data, evaluation results, and risk ratings in a standardized, auditable form, with the least custom build. Which AWS feature should they use?

options.text: Amazon SageMaker Model Cards.
options.explanation: Model Cards capture intended use, training details, evaluation, and risk information in a standardized, versioned, auditable document.

options.text: AWS CloudTrail.
options.explanation: CloudTrail records API activity, not model documentation and governance metadata.

options.text: AWS Config.
options.explanation: Config tracks resource configuration compliance, not model documentation.

options.text: A free-form README stored in S3.
options.explanation: A custom README lacks the standardized structure, versioning, and governance integration of Model Cards.

questions.question: A team is preparing a dataset to fine-tune a foundation model and wants to maximize resulting quality while avoiding overfitting and data leakage. Which TWO data-preparation steps best support these goals? (Select TWO.)

options.text: Deduplicate and clean/normalize the examples.
options.explanation: Removing duplicates and normalizing inconsistent text reduces bias toward repeated samples and improves the signal the model learns from.

options.text: Hold out a separate validation/test split that does not overlap the training data.
options.explanation: A non-overlapping evaluation split prevents train/test leakage and gives an honest measure of generalization.

options.text: Include duplicate records to increase the dataset size.
options.explanation: Duplication inflates size artificially and encourages overfitting to repeated examples.

options.text: Include PII in the examples to add more signal.
options.explanation: Including PII creates privacy and compliance risk and does not improve task quality.

options.text: Increase the number of training epochs regardless of validation loss.
options.explanation: Blindly adding epochs is a training hyperparameter choice, not data preparation, and risks overfitting.

questions.question: A real-time chat router must classify incoming messages into a small set of intents. The task is simple, but volume is very high and the team wants the lowest latency and cost. Which Amazon Nova model is MOST appropriate?

options.text: Amazon Nova Micro.
options.explanation: Nova Micro is the text-only, lowest-latency, lowest-cost Nova model, well suited to high-volume, simple text tasks such as intent classification.

options.text: Amazon Nova Pro.
options.explanation: Nova Pro is the most capable multimodal Nova model, with higher cost and latency than a simple classification task requires.

options.text: Amazon Nova Canvas.
options.explanation: Nova Canvas generates images and is not used for text classification.

options.text: Amazon Nova Reel.
options.explanation: Nova Reel generates video and is irrelevant to text intent classification.

questions.question: A company is satisfied with the accuracy of a large foundation model but needs lower inference cost and latency in production for a well-defined task. Which Amazon Bedrock capability is designed to transfer the larger model's quality to a smaller, cheaper model?

options.text: Model distillation, training a smaller student model from the larger teacher model.
options.explanation: Bedrock model distillation generates data from a teacher model to fine-tune a smaller student that approximates its quality at lower cost and latency.

options.text: Cross-Region inference.
options.explanation: Cross-Region inference distributes load across Regions for throughput; it does not reduce per-call model size or cost.

options.text: Provisioned Throughput.
options.explanation: Provisioned Throughput reserves capacity for a chosen model but does not create a smaller, cheaper model.

options.text: Prompt caching.
options.explanation: Prompt caching reuses repeated prompt prefixes but does not change the model used for inference.

questions.question: An assistant must answer using internal documents that change frequently, and answers must always reflect the latest content. Which approach meets this with the LOWEST ongoing maintenance?

options.text: Retrieval-Augmented Generation with a knowledge base over the documents.
options.explanation: RAG retrieves the current documents at query time, so updated content is reflected immediately without retraining the model.

options.text: Fine-tune the model nightly on the latest documents.
options.explanation: Repeated fine-tuning is costly and operationally heavy and still lags behind document changes between runs.

options.text: Use continued pre-training whenever documents change.
options.explanation: Continued pre-training is even heavier than fine-tuning and is not how you keep answers current.

options.text: Paste all documents into every prompt.
options.explanation: Stuffing all documents into the prompt is costly, hits context limits, and does not scale as the corpus grows.

questions.question: Prompt engineering has not reliably produced a company's required brand voice and a strict, consistent output format. The underlying domain knowledge is stable and does not change often. Which customization approach is MOST appropriate?

options.text: Fine-tune the model on labeled examples that demonstrate the desired tone and format.
options.explanation: Fine-tuning teaches consistent style and formatting from examples and is appropriate when the behavior, not freshness of facts, is the gap.

options.text: Add a knowledge base and use RAG.
options.explanation: RAG supplies fresh facts but does not reliably enforce tone and output formatting.

options.text: Increase the temperature parameter.
options.explanation: Higher temperature increases variability, working against a consistent voice and format.

options.text: Purchase Provisioned Throughput.
options.explanation: Provisioned Throughput affects capacity and cost, not the model's style or formatting behavior.

questions.question: A firm wants a model to deeply absorb specialized domain vocabulary and patterns from a very large corpus of unlabeled internal text. Which Amazon Bedrock customization method fits this goal?

options.text: Continued pre-training on the unlabeled corpus.
options.explanation: Continued pre-training adapts a model to domain language using large volumes of unlabeled text.

options.text: Supervised fine-tuning on labeled prompt/completion pairs.
options.explanation: Fine-tuning needs labeled examples and targets specific task behavior rather than broad domain-language adaptation.

options.text: Retrieval-Augmented Generation.
options.explanation: RAG injects retrieved context at inference time but does not change what the model has learned.

options.text: Model distillation.
options.explanation: Distillation compresses a larger model into a smaller one; it is not a method for absorbing a domain corpus.

questions.question: A compliance summarizer must produce highly consistent, low-variability output and avoid creative phrasing. Which TWO inference-parameter adjustments best support more deterministic, focused responses? (Select TWO.)

options.text: Lower the temperature.
options.explanation: A low temperature reduces randomness, making outputs more deterministic and repeatable.

options.text: Lower top-p (nucleus sampling).
options.explanation: A lower top-p narrows the token pool to the most probable tokens, reducing variability.

options.text: Raise the temperature substantially.
options.explanation: Higher temperature increases randomness, the opposite of the requirement.

options.text: Greatly increase top-k to consider many tokens.
options.explanation: A large top-k widens the candidate pool and increases variability.

options.text: Increase max tokens.
options.explanation: Max tokens controls output length, not determinism.

questions.question: A team must run sentiment analysis over 10 million archived product reviews. The results are needed for a weekly report, so latency does not matter, and cost should be minimized. Which Amazon Bedrock option fits best?

options.text: Amazon Bedrock batch inference.
options.explanation: Batch inference processes large datasets asynchronously at a lower price point, ideal for latency-tolerant, high-volume jobs.

options.text: A loop of real-time InvokeModel calls.
options.explanation: Per-record real-time calls are more expensive and unnecessary for a weekly, offline report.

options.text: Provisioned Throughput running continuously.
options.explanation: Reserved capacity running 24/7 wastes money for a once-weekly job.

options.text: Increase max tokens to process more reviews per call.
options.explanation: Max tokens does not batch records and would not reduce overall cost for this workload.

questions.question: A startup's Bedrock usage is spiky and unpredictable, with long idle periods between bursts. They want to pay only for what they use and avoid paying for idle capacity. Which pricing/inference choice is MOST cost-effective?

options.text: On-demand inference.
options.explanation: On-demand pricing charges per request with no commitment, which is most cost-effective for spiky, unpredictable, low-utilization traffic.

options.text: Provisioned Throughput with a 6-month commitment.
options.explanation: A commitment pays for reserved capacity that sits idle during the long quiet periods.

options.text: Batch inference for all traffic.
options.explanation: Batch is offline and cannot serve interactive, bursty requests.

options.text: A dedicated SageMaker endpoint kept always on.
options.explanation: An always-on endpoint incurs cost during idle periods, defeating the goal.

questions.question: A team must compare two candidate foundation models for a summarization use case across relevance and coherence at scale, without standing up a human rating panel. Which approach has the LEAST operational overhead?

options.text: Use an Amazon Bedrock model evaluation job with an LLM-as-a-judge (automatic) evaluator.
options.explanation: Bedrock model evaluation supports automatic LLM-as-a-judge scoring across quality dimensions at scale with minimal setup.

options.text: Recruit and manage a human evaluation panel for all outputs.
options.explanation: Human-only evaluation is accurate but operationally heavy and slow at scale.

options.text: Spot-check a handful of outputs manually in a notebook.
options.explanation: Ad hoc manual review is not systematic or scalable for a reliable comparison.

options.text: Compare CloudWatch latency metrics for the two models.
options.explanation: Latency metrics do not measure summarization relevance or coherence.

questions.question: A developer wants one consistent API to hold multi-turn conversations and use tool calling across several different Bedrock models, without writing model-specific request and response handling. Which API should they use?

options.text: The Amazon Bedrock Converse API.
options.explanation: Converse provides a unified, model-agnostic interface for multi-turn messages and tool use across supported Bedrock models.

options.text: A separate InvokeModel integration per model with custom payloads.
options.explanation: Per-model InvokeModel payloads require model-specific request/response handling, which Converse abstracts away.

options.text: Custom adapter code in front of each model.
options.explanation: Building adapters reinvents what the Converse API already standardizes.

options.text: Amazon API Gateway request mapping templates.
options.explanation: API Gateway mapping does not normalize differing model message and tool-use formats.

questions.question: An application already calls a supported Bedrock model and the team wants to reduce token-generation latency with the smallest possible application change. Which option should they evaluate first?

options.text: Enable latency-optimized inference for the supported model.
options.explanation: Latency-optimized inference reduces response latency for supported models without an application rewrite.

options.text: Switch to a larger, more capable model.
options.explanation: A larger model typically increases latency rather than reducing it.

options.text: Move the workload to batch inference.
options.explanation: Batch inference is asynchronous and increases, not decreases, time-to-response.

options.text: Add more retries to the client.
options.explanation: Retries handle failures; they do not lower per-request latency.

questions.question: A company has a fine-tuned open-weights model (a supported architecture) trained outside AWS and wants to serve it through the standard Amazon Bedrock APIs on demand, without operating its own inference servers. Which capability should it use?

options.text: Amazon Bedrock Custom Model Import.
options.explanation: Custom Model Import lets you bring supported custom model weights into Bedrock and invoke them through the standard on-demand Bedrock APIs without managing servers.

options.text: A self-managed Amazon SageMaker real-time endpoint.
options.explanation: A SageMaker endpoint can serve the model but requires managing the endpoint and does not expose it through the Bedrock APIs.

options.text: A container on Amazon ECS.
options.explanation: Running the model on ECS means operating your own inference infrastructure.

options.text: Provisioned Throughput on a Bedrock base model.
options.explanation: Provisioned Throughput reserves capacity for Bedrock models, not for importing your own weights.

questions.question: An application must accept product photos from users and answer natural-language questions about what is in each image. Which choice is MOST appropriate?

options.text: Invoke a Bedrock multimodal foundation model (such as Claude or Amazon Nova Pro) through the Converse API.
options.explanation: A multimodal FM can reason over image inputs and produce natural-language answers about their content.

options.text: Use Amazon Rekognition to return object labels.
options.explanation: Rekognition returns labels and detections but does not hold a natural-language Q&A about an image.

options.text: Use Amazon Textract to read the image.
options.explanation: Textract extracts text from documents and forms; it does not answer visual questions about general photos.

options.text: Use Amazon Titan Text Embeddings.
options.explanation: Text embeddings represent text for search; they do not analyze image content or answer questions.

questions.question: A team is building semantic search and wants a text embedding model that lets them choose smaller output dimensions to cut vector-storage cost while retaining strong retrieval quality. Which model fits?

options.text: Amazon Titan Text Embeddings V2, which supports configurable output dimensions.
options.explanation: Titan Text Embeddings V2 lets you select among multiple output dimension sizes to trade storage/cost against accuracy.

options.text: Amazon Titan Image Generator.
options.explanation: An image generation model does not produce text embeddings for search.

options.text: Anthropic Claude.
options.explanation: Claude is a text-generation model, not a dedicated embeddings model.

options.text: Amazon Comprehend.
options.explanation: Comprehend provides NLP analyses, not configurable embedding vectors for a vector store.

questions.question: A team is creating an Amazon Bedrock knowledge base and wants a fully managed vector store that integrates natively with Bedrock, supports frequent low-latency similarity queries, and requires no cluster sizing or management. Which option fits best?

options.text: Amazon OpenSearch Serverless as the vector store.
options.explanation: OpenSearch Serverless is a natively integrated, fully managed vector store for Bedrock knowledge bases that scales automatically for frequent low-latency queries.

options.text: A self-managed Amazon OpenSearch Service cluster you size and patch.
options.explanation: A provisioned cluster requires capacity planning, scaling, and maintenance, adding operational overhead.

options.text: Amazon DynamoDB with a custom similarity function.
options.explanation: DynamoDB has no native vector similarity search and would require custom, slower logic.

options.text: Amazon S3 with objects named by embedding.
options.explanation: Plain S3 has no vector index or similarity search capability.

questions.question: A knowledge base serves several departments, and each user must only retrieve documents tagged for their department. The team wants to enforce this at retrieval time without maintaining separate copies of the corpus. Which capability should they use?

options.text: Apply metadata filtering on the Retrieve call using the document's department attribute.
options.explanation: Bedrock knowledge bases support metadata attributes and filter expressions so retrieval returns only chunks matching the user's department.

options.text: Add an instruction in the prompt asking the model to ignore other departments.
options.explanation: Prompt instructions cannot reliably prevent irrelevant or unauthorized chunks from being retrieved and used.

options.text: Raise the temperature so the model is less literal.
options.explanation: Temperature has nothing to do with which documents are retrieved.

options.text: Retrieve everything and truncate the results in the client.
options.explanation: Truncation does not enforce department-level access and may still expose other departments' content.

questions.question: An application must show end users the exact source passages that support each generated answer, with the least custom development. Which Amazon Bedrock approach provides this?

options.text: Use the RetrieveAndGenerate API, which returns generated answers with citations to the source chunks.
options.explanation: RetrieveAndGenerate produces a grounded answer and returns citations identifying the retrieved passages used, with no custom attribution code.

options.text: Use InvokeModel with the whole corpus pasted into the prompt.
options.explanation: Stuffing the corpus into the prompt does not produce reliable citations and does not scale.

options.text: Enable model invocation logging and show users the logs.
options.explanation: Invocation logs are for operators and do not provide user-facing source citations.

options.text: Increase max tokens so the model lists its sources.
options.explanation: Asking the model to self-report sources is unreliable; max tokens does not create verifiable citations.

questions.question: A developer wants to fetch the most relevant chunks from a Bedrock knowledge base and then run them through their own custom prompt and a model of their choosing. Which API is appropriate?

options.text: The Retrieve API, which returns ranked chunks for the developer to use in a custom generation step.
options.explanation: Retrieve returns the relevant passages only, letting the developer control the prompt and generation model themselves.

options.text: The RetrieveAndGenerate API exclusively.
options.explanation: RetrieveAndGenerate performs the generation for you, which removes the custom prompt/model control the developer wants.

options.text: The StartIngestionJob API.
options.explanation: StartIngestionJob ingests data into the knowledge base; it does not retrieve chunks for a query.

options.text: The CreateModelCustomizationJob API.
options.explanation: That API runs fine-tuning, unrelated to retrieving chunks.

questions.question: A RAG assistant occasionally produces answers that are not supported by the retrieved passages. The team wants a managed way to detect and block responses that are not grounded in the source context. Which capability helps MOST?

options.text: Enable the Amazon Bedrock Guardrails contextual grounding check.
options.explanation: The contextual grounding (and relevance) check scores whether a response is grounded in the provided source and can block ungrounded answers.

options.text: Increase the model temperature.
options.explanation: Higher temperature increases variability and tends to worsen, not reduce, ungrounded output.

options.text: Switch to on-demand pricing.
options.explanation: Pricing mode has no effect on grounding.

options.text: Remove the retrieved context from the prompt.
options.explanation: Removing context guarantees the answer cannot be grounded, the opposite of the goal.

questions.question: A multi-tenant SaaS uses one shared knowledge base and must guarantee that one tenant can never retrieve another tenant's documents. Which design is the MOST secure and reliable?

options.text: Tag each document with its tenant ID and apply a metadata filter derived from the authenticated tenant on every Retrieve request (or use a separate knowledge base per tenant).
options.explanation: Enforcing a server-side metadata filter bound to the verified tenant identity (or full isolation via per-tenant knowledge bases) reliably prevents cross-tenant retrieval.

options.text: Instruct the model in the system prompt to only answer about the current tenant.
options.explanation: Prompt instructions do not control retrieval and can be bypassed, so other tenants' chunks can still be retrieved.

options.text: Let the client pass the tenant filter and trust it as-is.
options.explanation: A client-supplied, untrusted filter can be tampered with to access other tenants' data.

options.text: Store all tenants together with no filter and rely on ranking.
options.explanation: Relevance ranking can still surface another tenant's documents; it is not an isolation mechanism.

questions.question: An analyst tool must answer questions that require reasoning over relationships among connected entities (people, companies, and the links between them), not just similarity to text passages. Which Amazon Bedrock knowledge base option is designed for this?

options.text: A knowledge base using GraphRAG with Amazon Neptune Analytics.
options.explanation: GraphRAG with Neptune Analytics builds a graph of entities and relationships, enabling retrieval that reasons over connections rather than only vector similarity.

options.text: A standard vector-only knowledge base.
options.explanation: Pure vector similarity does not capture explicit relationships among connected entities.

options.text: An Amazon DynamoDB table of entities.
options.explanation: DynamoDB stores items but provides no graph reasoning or managed RAG integration.

options.text: Amazon Athena queries over CSV files.
options.explanation: Athena runs SQL over files and is not a graph-aware retrieval system for RAG.

questions.question: Business users want to ask natural-language analytical questions (sums, trends, comparisons) over data in an Amazon Redshift warehouse, and have the system generate and run the appropriate queries. Which Amazon Bedrock capability fits?

options.text: Amazon Bedrock Knowledge Bases with structured data retrieval, which generates SQL from natural language.
options.explanation: Structured-data knowledge bases translate natural-language questions into SQL against the connected warehouse, returning accurate analytical results.

options.text: Embed every table row as a vector and do similarity search.
options.explanation: Vector similarity over rows cannot perform aggregations or precise analytical queries.

options.text: Use Amazon Comprehend to read the question.
options.explanation: Comprehend analyzes text but does not generate SQL or query a warehouse.

options.text: Fine-tune a model on the warehouse schema.
options.explanation: Fine-tuning does not give live, accurate query results over changing data the way text-to-SQL retrieval does.

questions.question: A company keeps 200 million archived document embeddings that are queried only a few times per month for occasional investigations. They want the lowest-cost durable vector storage with no capacity to manage. Which option is MOST cost-effective?

options.text: Amazon S3 Vectors.
options.explanation: S3 Vectors offers serverless, low-cost vector storage and search at massive scale, ideal for large corpora queried infrequently with pay-for-use pricing.

options.text: A provisioned OpenSearch Service cluster sized for the full corpus.
options.explanation: A continuously provisioned cluster is expensive for data queried only a few times a month.

options.text: OpenSearch Serverless kept warm at all times.
options.explanation: Keeping high-performance serverless capacity ready is more expensive than needed for rare queries.

options.text: Amazon RDS for PostgreSQL with pgvector.
options.explanation: RDS requires running instances and is not the lowest-cost option for rarely queried, very large vector sets.

questions.question: A team has standardized on Amazon Aurora PostgreSQL for its application data and wants to store and query embeddings alongside related relational records using familiar tooling, while keeping the vector store as a managed Bedrock knowledge base option. Which choice fits best?

options.text: Use Amazon Aurora PostgreSQL with the pgvector extension as the knowledge base vector store.
options.explanation: Aurora PostgreSQL with pgvector is a supported Bedrock vector store and lets the team keep vectors next to relational data using PostgreSQL tooling.

options.text: Adopt a brand-new self-managed search cluster instead.
options.explanation: Introducing a separate self-managed system adds operational overhead the team is trying to avoid.

options.text: Store vectors in Amazon DynamoDB.
options.explanation: DynamoDB is not a supported vector store and lacks native similarity search.

options.text: Keep embeddings only in application memory.
options.explanation: In-memory storage is not durable and cannot serve as a knowledge base vector store.

questions.question: Source content lives in Amazon S3, Confluence, and a SharePoint site. The team wants to ingest all of it into one Bedrock knowledge base with the LEAST custom code. What should they do?

options.text: Use the built-in Bedrock knowledge base data source connectors for S3, Confluence, and SharePoint.
options.explanation: Bedrock knowledge bases provide managed connectors for sources such as S3, Confluence, SharePoint, Salesforce, and web crawlers, avoiding custom ingestion code.

options.text: Write custom crawlers for each system and push to S3 only.
options.explanation: Custom crawlers add development and maintenance overhead that the built-in connectors remove.

options.text: Manually export everything to S3 on a schedule.
options.explanation: Manual exports are brittle and lag behind source changes compared with managed connectors.

options.text: Fine-tune the model on each source instead of ingesting.
options.explanation: Fine-tuning is not how you ingest changing documents for retrieval.

questions.question: A chat experience built on RetrieveAndGenerate must keep context across follow-up questions in a conversation with the least custom state management. Which approach should the developer use?

options.text: Pass a session identifier to RetrieveAndGenerate so Bedrock maintains the conversation context.
options.explanation: RetrieveAndGenerate supports a session ID that preserves multi-turn context without the developer building custom conversation state.

options.text: Re-embed the entire chat history into the vector store each turn.
options.explanation: Re-embedding the conversation each turn is unnecessary and costly and is not how session context is maintained.

options.text: Store every message in DynamoDB and rebuild the prompt manually each turn.
options.explanation: Manual state assembly adds development overhead compared with built-in session support.

options.text: Increase the model context window only.
options.explanation: A larger window does not automatically carry prior turns into each RetrieveAndGenerate call.

questions.question: Users ask complex, multi-part questions, and the knowledge base often retrieves passages for only one part of the question, producing incomplete answers. Which built-in capability best addresses this?

options.text: Enable query decomposition (query reformulation) so the knowledge base breaks complex questions into sub-queries.
options.explanation: Query decomposition splits a multi-part question into focused sub-queries, retrieving relevant passages for each part and improving completeness.

options.text: Increase the model temperature.
options.explanation: Temperature affects generation randomness, not how queries are retrieved.

options.text: Switch to a larger generation model only.
options.explanation: A larger model cannot answer using passages that retrieval never returned.

options.text: Reduce the number of retrieved results.
options.explanation: Returning fewer results would make incomplete coverage worse, not better.

questions.question: A team wants to add retrieval-augmented answers over their documents but does not want to build and operate an embedding pipeline, a vector database, and custom retrieval code. Which option provides managed RAG with the LEAST operational overhead?

options.text: Use Amazon Bedrock Knowledge Bases, which manages embedding, storage, and retrieval as a service.
options.explanation: Knowledge Bases provide fully managed RAG, handling chunking, embeddings, the vector store, and retrieval so the team avoids building the pipeline.

options.text: Build a custom pipeline with Lambda, a self-managed vector database, and bespoke retrieval code.
options.explanation: A hand-built pipeline maximizes operational overhead, which the team is trying to avoid.

options.text: Fine-tune a model on the documents instead of retrieving.
options.explanation: Fine-tuning does not keep answers current as documents change and is not managed RAG.

options.text: Paste documents into prompts at request time.
options.explanation: Prompt stuffing does not scale and is not a managed retrieval solution.
