questions.question: Long policy documents have distinct topical sections. With the default fixed-size chunking, related ideas are split across chunks and retrieval quality suffers. Which chunking strategy is MOST likely to improve relevance?

options.text: Semantic chunking, which splits on semantic boundaries so each chunk holds a coherent idea.
options.explanation: Semantic chunking groups text by meaning rather than a fixed token count, keeping related content together and improving retrieval relevance.

options.text: Disable chunking and embed each whole document as one vector.
options.explanation: Embedding an entire long document dilutes the vector and harms precise retrieval.

options.text: Use larger fixed-size chunks only.
options.explanation: Bigger fixed chunks still ignore topic boundaries and can mix unrelated content.

options.text: Increase the model temperature.
options.explanation: Temperature affects generation, not how documents are chunked or retrieved.

questions.question: A team needs precise matching on small text units but also wants the language model to receive surrounding context when a small unit matches. Which chunking approach provides both?

options.text: Hierarchical (parent-child) chunking.
options.explanation: Hierarchical chunking matches on small child chunks for precision and supplies the larger parent chunk as context for generation.

options.text: A single fixed chunk size for everything.
options.explanation: One fixed size cannot simultaneously optimize for precise matching and broad context.

options.text: No chunking at all.
options.explanation: Treating each document as one chunk loses the precision of small-unit matching.

options.text: Switching to a larger embedding dimension.
options.explanation: Dimension size does not provide the parent-context behavior hierarchical chunking gives.

questions.question: Answers are frequently cut off because relevant sentences fall right at the boundary between two chunks. Which adjustment most directly addresses this?

options.text: Add chunk overlap so adjacent chunks share boundary text.
options.explanation: Overlap repeats text across chunk boundaries so information spanning a boundary is captured in at least one chunk.

options.text: Remove all overlap to keep chunks distinct.
options.explanation: Removing overlap makes boundary loss worse, not better.

options.text: Increase the temperature.
options.explanation: Temperature does not affect chunk boundaries.

options.text: Use a larger generation model.
options.explanation: A larger model cannot recover content that retrieval split across boundaries.

questions.question: A team decides to upgrade to a newer embedding model for better retrieval. What must they do to keep retrieval working correctly?

options.text: Re-embed (re-index) the entire document corpus with the new model so document and query vectors share the same space.
options.explanation: Query and document embeddings must come from the same model; changing the model requires re-embedding the whole corpus to avoid vector-space mismatch.

options.text: Do nothing; existing vectors remain compatible.
options.explanation: Vectors from a different model live in a different space and will not match new query embeddings.

options.text: Re-embed only the queries, not the documents.
options.explanation: Mismatched query and document embeddings produce poor or empty retrieval.

options.text: Lower the temperature on the generation model.
options.explanation: Temperature is unrelated to embedding compatibility.

questions.question: A vector store is becoming expensive as the corpus grows. The team wants to cut storage and query cost while keeping acceptable retrieval accuracy. Which embedding choice helps MOST?

options.text: Use an embedding model that supports smaller output dimensions (for example, Titan Text Embeddings V2) and select a smaller dimension.
options.explanation: Smaller-dimension embeddings reduce index size and query cost, and V2 lets you trade some accuracy for substantial storage savings.

options.text: Always use the maximum available embedding dimension.
options.explanation: Maximum dimensions increase storage and cost, the opposite of the goal.

options.text: Store each chunk twice for redundancy.
options.explanation: Duplicating vectors increases cost without improving accuracy.

options.text: Raise the generation temperature.
options.explanation: Temperature does not affect embedding storage or cost.

questions.question: Evaluation shows the correct passage exists in the store but is sometimes not returned for a query, so the model lacks the information to answer. Which retrieval-setting change should the team try FIRST?

options.text: Increase the number of results (top-k) returned for each query.
options.explanation: Returning more candidate chunks raises recall so the correct passage is more likely to be included for generation.

options.text: Decrease the number of results returned.
options.explanation: Returning fewer results lowers recall and makes misses more likely.

options.text: Increase the temperature.
options.explanation: Temperature affects generation, not which chunks are retrieved.

options.text: Shorten the user's question automatically.
options.explanation: Arbitrarily shortening the query can remove signal needed for retrieval.

questions.question: Chunks were configured to be very large, and retrieval now returns passages where the relevant sentence is buried among unrelated text, weakening match quality. Which change is MOST appropriate?

options.text: Reduce the chunk size so each chunk represents a more focused unit of meaning.
options.explanation: Smaller, focused chunks produce embeddings that better represent specific content, improving match precision.

options.text: Increase the chunk size further.
options.explanation: Even larger chunks dilute the embedding more and worsen precision.

options.text: Embed the whole document as one chunk.
options.explanation: A single document-level vector is the extreme case of dilution and reduces precision.

options.text: Raise the temperature.
options.explanation: Temperature is unrelated to chunk granularity.

questions.question: A knowledge base ingests a large set of short, self-contained FAQ entries, where each file already represents one complete question-and-answer. Which chunking choice is MOST appropriate?

options.text: Use a no-chunking strategy so each short file is treated as a single chunk.
options.explanation: When each file is already an atomic, self-contained unit, no chunking preserves the complete meaning per entry without splitting it.

options.text: Apply aggressive small fixed-size chunking to every file.
options.explanation: Splitting already-atomic FAQ entries fragments coherent answers and harms retrieval.

options.text: Merge all FAQ files into one chunk.
options.explanation: Combining unrelated entries into one chunk dilutes each entry's embedding.

options.text: Increase embedding dimensions to compensate.
options.explanation: Dimension size does not fix inappropriately split or merged atomic entries.

questions.question: An application must retrieve relevant items from a combined catalog of product images and text descriptions using a single query that may be text or an image. Which embedding approach supports this?

options.text: Use a multimodal embedding model (such as Amazon Titan Multimodal Embeddings) so images and text share one vector space.
options.explanation: Multimodal embeddings place images and text in a shared space, enabling cross-modal similarity search from either input type.

options.text: Use a text-only embedding model for everything.
options.explanation: A text-only model cannot embed images for similarity search.

options.text: Use Amazon Rekognition labels as the only index.
options.explanation: Label detection is not a shared embedding space for semantic text-and-image retrieval.

options.text: Store images as base64 strings and embed the strings as text.
options.explanation: Embedding raw base64 as text does not capture visual semantics for retrieval.

questions.question: A team wants to raise the overall quality of what their RAG system retrieves and ultimately answers. Which TWO techniques are most effective? (Select TWO.)

options.text: Choose a chunking strategy (semantic or hierarchical) aligned to the documents' structure.
options.explanation: Chunking that respects document structure produces more coherent, relevant chunks and better retrieval.

options.text: Add a reranking step to reorder retrieved candidates by query relevance.
options.explanation: A reranker reorders the initial candidate set so the most relevant passages are used for generation.

options.text: Raise the generation temperature to add variety.
options.explanation: Higher temperature increases randomness and does not improve retrieval quality.

options.text: Increase max tokens on the response.
options.explanation: Output length does not change which passages are retrieved or how relevant they are.

options.text: Always return the fewest possible results.
options.explanation: Minimizing results can drop relevant passages and reduce answer quality.

questions.question: A team wants to A/B test two wordings of a production prompt and roll back instantly if one underperforms, without redeploying application code. Which Amazon Bedrock capability supports this?

options.text: Amazon Bedrock Prompt Management with versioned prompts and variants.
options.explanation: Prompt Management stores prompts as versioned, parameterized resources with variants, so teams can compare wordings and switch or roll back without code changes.

options.text: Hard-code both prompts in the application and redeploy to switch.
options.explanation: Hard-coding requires a code change and redeploy for every switch or rollback.

options.text: Change the temperature parameter to switch prompts.
options.explanation: Temperature does not select between prompt wordings.

options.text: Store prompts in environment variables only.
options.explanation: Environment variables lack versioning, variants, and managed rollback for prompts.

questions.question: A developer must cap the length of model responses to control cost and prevent overly long outputs in a chat feature. Which inference setting should they use?

options.text: Set the maxTokens (maximum output tokens) parameter.
options.explanation: maxTokens bounds the number of tokens the model can generate, directly limiting response length and cost.

options.text: Raise the temperature.
options.explanation: Temperature changes randomness, not output length or cost ceiling.

options.text: Increase top-k.
options.explanation: Top-k widens token choice diversity; it does not bound length.

options.text: Add more stop sequences for unrelated phrases.
options.explanation: Unrelated stop sequences do not reliably cap general response length.

questions.question: An assistant must consistently follow durable behavior rules (role, tone, safety constraints) across every turn of a conversation, separate from each user's question. Where should these durable instructions be placed?

options.text: In the system prompt.
options.explanation: The system prompt sets persistent instructions and context that govern the model's behavior across the conversation, distinct from per-turn user messages.

options.text: Appended to each user message by the user.
options.explanation: Relying on users to repeat rules is unreliable and inconsistent.

options.text: In the temperature setting.
options.explanation: Temperature is a sampling parameter, not a place for behavior instructions.

options.text: In the stop sequences list.
options.explanation: Stop sequences end generation; they do not encode behavior rules.

questions.question: Outputs must follow a precise structured format, and instructions alone have not produced consistent results. Which prompt-engineering technique most improves format adherence?

options.text: Provide a few worked examples (few-shot exemplars) demonstrating the exact desired format.
options.explanation: Few-shot examples show the model the precise structure to imitate, improving consistency beyond instructions alone.

options.text: Increase the temperature for more creative formatting.
options.explanation: Higher temperature increases variability and undermines consistent formatting.

options.text: Remove all instructions and let the model decide.
options.explanation: Removing guidance reduces, not improves, format adherence.

options.text: Shorten the prompt to a single sentence.
options.explanation: Over-shortening removes the structure cues the model needs.

questions.question: Several applications reuse the same prompt with only a few values changing per request (customer name, product, locale). The team wants to manage this prompt centrally and inject values at runtime. Which approach fits best?

options.text: Use Amazon Bedrock Prompt Management templates with input variables.
options.explanation: Prompt Management supports parameterized templates with input variables, so a single managed prompt is reused and values are supplied at invocation time.

options.text: Copy the prompt text into each application and edit by hand.
options.explanation: Duplicated, hand-edited prompts drift apart and are hard to maintain or update centrally.

options.text: Concatenate strings ad hoc in every service.
options.explanation: Ad hoc concatenation gives no central governance, versioning, or reuse.

options.text: Raise the temperature to make the prompt flexible.
options.explanation: Temperature does not parameterize or manage prompts.

questions.question: A developer needs to orchestrate a multi-step generative workflow (classify input, retrieve context, then generate a response) with branching, and wants a managed, low-code way to build and version it. Which Amazon Bedrock feature is designed for this?

options.text: Amazon Bedrock Flows (prompt flows).
options.explanation: Bedrock Flows let you visually build and version multi-step, branching generative workflows that chain prompts, knowledge bases, and other components with little code.

options.text: A single InvokeModel call.
options.explanation: One model call cannot orchestrate a multi-step, branching workflow.

options.text: Manually wiring Lambda functions for every step from scratch.
options.explanation: Hand-built orchestration adds development and maintenance overhead versus a managed flow builder.

options.text: Increasing max tokens.
options.explanation: Output length has nothing to do with workflow orchestration.

questions.question: For a regression test suite, a team needs the same prompt to return as close to the same output as possible on each run. Which single inference parameter change most increases determinism?

options.text: Set the temperature to 0 (or very near 0).
options.explanation: A temperature at or near 0 makes sampling effectively greedy, producing the most consistent, repeatable outputs.

options.text: Set a very high top-k.
options.explanation: A high top-k widens the sampling pool and increases variability.

options.text: Increase max tokens.
options.explanation: Output length does not control run-to-run consistency.

options.text: Raise the temperature to 1.
options.explanation: A high temperature maximizes randomness, the opposite of determinism.

questions.question: In a RAG assistant, the team wants to reduce fabricated answers by making the model rely only on the supplied context and admit when the answer is not present. Which prompt technique helps MOST?

options.text: Instruct the model to answer only from the provided context and to say it does not know when the context lacks the answer.
options.explanation: Explicit grounding instructions plus an allowed 'I don't know' response reduce fabrication when the retrieved context does not contain the answer.

options.text: Raise the temperature so answers are more creative.
options.explanation: More creativity increases the chance of fabricated content.

options.text: Remove the retrieved context to keep prompts short.
options.explanation: Without context the model must guess, increasing hallucination.

options.text: Tell the model to always produce an answer no matter what.
options.explanation: Forcing an answer encourages fabrication when information is missing.

questions.question: A developer wants to prevent unbounded, runaway generations that drive up cost and sometimes ramble past the intended answer. Which TWO inference controls directly help? (Select TWO.)

options.text: Set a sensible maxTokens limit.
options.explanation: maxTokens caps how many tokens can be generated, bounding cost and preventing runaway length.

options.text: Define stop sequences that end generation at a known marker.
options.explanation: Stop sequences halt generation when a defined phrase appears, preventing the model from rambling past the answer.

options.text: Increase the temperature.
options.explanation: Higher temperature does not limit length and can make output more verbose and variable.

options.text: Increase top-k substantially.
options.explanation: A larger top-k affects token diversity, not output length.

options.text: Enlarge the context window.
options.explanation: A bigger input window does not constrain how long the output can be.

questions.question: A developer is building an Amazon Bedrock agent that must call an internal order-management API to look up and update orders. What is the standard way to give the agent this capability?

options.text: Define an action group backed by an AWS Lambda function, described with an API (OpenAPI) or function schema the agent can call.
options.explanation: Bedrock agents use action groups: a schema declares the available operations and a Lambda function executes them when the agent decides to call the API.

options.text: Paste the API documentation into the agent's instructions and hope it calls the API.
options.explanation: Instructions alone do not give the agent an executable tool; action groups provide the actual invocation path.

options.text: Increase the model's temperature so it can reach the API.
options.explanation: Temperature has nothing to do with tool or API access.

options.text: Store the API responses in the prompt as static text.
options.explanation: Static text cannot perform live lookups or updates against the order system.

questions.question: For compliance, an organization wants its own application to execute tool/API calls rather than letting the Bedrock agent invoke a Lambda directly, while still letting the agent decide which action to take. Which agent capability supports this?

options.text: Configure the action group to return control to the caller so the application performs the action and returns results to the agent.
options.explanation: Return of control hands the chosen action and parameters back to the calling application to execute, instead of the agent invoking a Lambda directly.

options.text: Disable the agent's reasoning entirely.
options.explanation: Disabling reasoning would remove the agent's ability to decide which action to take.

options.text: Hard-code the action in the system prompt.
options.explanation: A hard-coded action removes the agent's dynamic decision-making and is not the return-of-control mechanism.

options.text: Lower the temperature to 0.
options.explanation: Temperature does not control where tool execution happens.

questions.question: An agent should ground its answers in the company's policy documents in addition to taking actions. With the LEAST custom code, how should the developer add this knowledge?

options.text: Associate an Amazon Bedrock knowledge base with the agent.
options.explanation: Associating a knowledge base lets the agent retrieve and ground responses on the documents without custom retrieval code.

options.text: Embed the entire policy library into the agent's instructions.
options.explanation: Instructions have limited size and cannot scale to a full document library or stay current.

options.text: Fine-tune the agent's model on the policies each week.
options.explanation: Repeated fine-tuning is heavy and unnecessary when a knowledge base provides current grounding.

options.text: Store policies in DynamoDB and query them with custom code.
options.explanation: A custom store and retrieval code adds work that the built-in knowledge base association avoids.

questions.question: A team is deploying agents on Amazon Bedrock AgentCore and wants each user's agent to remember preferences and prior context across separate sessions, as a managed capability. Which AgentCore component should they use?

options.text: AgentCore Memory.
options.explanation: AgentCore Memory provides managed short-term and long-term memory so agents can persist and recall context across sessions.

options.text: AgentCore Browser.
options.explanation: The Browser tool provides managed web browsing, not persistent agent memory.

options.text: AgentCore Code Interpreter.
options.explanation: Code Interpreter runs code in a sandbox; it does not store cross-session memory.

options.text: AgentCore Gateway.
options.explanation: Gateway exposes APIs as tools; it is not the memory store.

questions.question: A company already has many internal REST APIs and Lambda functions and wants to expose them to its agents as secure, governed tools without writing custom integration code for each one. Which AgentCore capability is designed for this?

options.text: AgentCore Gateway.
options.explanation: AgentCore Gateway turns existing APIs, Lambda functions, and services into agent-ready tools (for example via MCP) with built-in authentication and governance.

options.text: AgentCore Memory.
options.explanation: Memory persists context; it does not expose APIs as tools.

options.text: AgentCore Code Interpreter.
options.explanation: Code Interpreter executes code, not API-to-tool exposure.

options.text: AgentCore Observability.
options.explanation: Observability provides tracing and monitoring, not tool integration.

questions.question: An analytics agent must run user-provided Python to perform calculations and data transformations safely, isolated from other workloads. Which AgentCore capability should the developer use?

options.text: AgentCore Code Interpreter.
options.explanation: Code Interpreter gives the agent a secure, isolated sandbox to execute code for calculations and data processing.

options.text: AgentCore Gateway.
options.explanation: Gateway exposes external tools; it is not a code execution sandbox.

options.text: AgentCore Browser.
options.explanation: Browser automates web interactions, not arbitrary code execution.

options.text: AgentCore Memory.
options.explanation: Memory stores context and does not execute code.

questions.question: An agent needs to navigate websites and interact with web pages to complete tasks, in a managed and secure environment rather than self-hosted browser infrastructure. Which AgentCore capability fits?

options.text: AgentCore Browser.
options.explanation: The AgentCore Browser tool provides a managed, secure, cloud-based browser the agent can use to navigate and interact with web pages.

options.text: AgentCore Code Interpreter.
options.explanation: Code Interpreter runs code; it is not a managed browser.

options.text: AgentCore Memory.
options.explanation: Memory persists context, not web interaction.

options.text: AgentCore Identity.
options.explanation: Identity manages secure access to resources, not browser automation.

questions.question: Agents must securely access third-party SaaS services on behalf of users, including handling tokens and consent, without the developer building a custom credential broker. Which AgentCore capability addresses this?

options.text: AgentCore Identity.
options.explanation: AgentCore Identity provides secure identity and access management for agents, including authenticating to and accessing AWS and third-party services on a user's behalf.

options.text: AgentCore Browser.
options.explanation: Browser automates web pages; it is not an identity and access broker.

options.text: AgentCore Memory.
options.explanation: Memory persists context, not credentials and consent flows.

options.text: AgentCore Code Interpreter.
options.explanation: Code Interpreter executes code and does not manage outbound identity.

questions.question: A complex workflow needs one coordinating agent to delegate subtasks to several specialized agents (billing, shipping, returns) and combine their results. Which Amazon Bedrock approach supports this?

options.text: Use multi-agent collaboration with a supervisor agent coordinating specialized sub-agents.
options.explanation: Bedrock multi-agent collaboration lets a supervisor agent route subtasks to specialized agents and aggregate their outputs.

options.text: Put all capabilities into a single prompt and hope the model separates them.
options.explanation: One monolithic prompt does not provide structured delegation or specialization.

options.text: Run unrelated agents with no coordination.
options.explanation: Without coordination there is no way to combine sub-agent results into one workflow.

options.text: Increase max tokens so one agent can do everything.
options.explanation: Output length does not provide multi-agent delegation.

questions.question: A developer wants an open-source, model-driven framework to build agents with tools and reasoning in a few lines of Python, and then deploy them to Amazon Bedrock AgentCore Runtime. Which option fits?

options.text: The Strands Agents SDK.
options.explanation: Strands Agents is AWS's open-source, model-driven agent SDK for building tool-using agents that can be deployed to AgentCore Runtime.

options.text: Amazon QuickSight.
options.explanation: QuickSight is a business-intelligence service, not an agent framework.

options.text: Amazon Comprehend.
options.explanation: Comprehend is an NLP service, not an agent-building SDK.

options.text: AWS Glue.
options.explanation: Glue is an ETL service and is unrelated to building agents.

questions.question: An operations team must debug why an agent chose a particular tool and trace each step, tool call, and latency in production. Which AgentCore capability provides this visibility?

options.text: AgentCore Observability, which provides tracing and metrics for agent steps and tool calls (integrating with CloudWatch/OpenTelemetry).
options.explanation: AgentCore Observability captures detailed traces of an agent's reasoning steps and tool invocations for debugging and monitoring.

options.text: AgentCore Memory.
options.explanation: Memory stores context; it is not a tracing and monitoring tool.

options.text: AgentCore Gateway.
options.explanation: Gateway exposes tools; it does not provide step-level tracing.

options.text: Raising the temperature.
options.explanation: Temperature is unrelated to observability.

questions.question: An agent can issue refunds, and the business requires a human to approve before any refund is executed. Which Bedrock agent feature enforces this in the action flow?

options.text: Enable user confirmation (require confirmation) on the sensitive action so the agent must obtain approval before executing it.
options.explanation: Bedrock agents can require user confirmation for an action, pausing for explicit approval before the operation runs.

options.text: Rely on the system prompt to ask the model to be careful.
options.explanation: A prompt request does not enforce a hard approval gate before execution.

options.text: Set temperature to 0 so the agent never makes mistakes.
options.explanation: Temperature does not create an approval step.

options.text: Disable the action group entirely.
options.explanation: Disabling the action removes the capability rather than gating it with approval.

questions.question: An application needs synchronous, low-latency predictions for small payloads with steady, predictable traffic. Which Amazon SageMaker AI deployment option is the best fit?

options.text: A real-time inference endpoint.
options.explanation: Real-time endpoints provide persistent, low-latency synchronous inference, ideal for steady traffic with small payloads.

options.text: A batch transform job.
options.explanation: Batch transform is for offline, asynchronous scoring of datasets, not low-latency synchronous requests.

options.text: An asynchronous inference endpoint.
options.explanation: Asynchronous endpoints suit long-running requests and large payloads, adding queueing latency unnecessary for small, fast requests.

options.text: A SageMaker Ground Truth job.
options.explanation: Ground Truth is for data labeling, not model serving.

questions.question: A model serves intermittent traffic with idle periods, does not require a GPU, and the team can tolerate occasional cold starts. They want to avoid paying for idle capacity. Which option is MOST cost-effective?

options.text: SageMaker Serverless Inference.
options.explanation: Serverless Inference automatically provisions and scales (including to zero) for intermittent CPU workloads, so you pay only when serving requests.

options.text: A large always-on real-time endpoint.
options.explanation: An always-on endpoint incurs cost during the long idle periods.

options.text: An asynchronous endpoint sized for peak.
options.explanation: Provisioning for peak wastes capacity for intermittent traffic.

options.text: A 24/7 multi-GPU instance.
options.explanation: A continuously running GPU instance is the most expensive and is unnecessary for a CPU workload.

questions.question: A company must host hundreds of small, similar models (one per customer) cost-effectively behind as few endpoints as possible, loading each model on demand. Which SageMaker feature fits best?

options.text: A multi-model endpoint (MME).
options.explanation: Multi-model endpoints host many models behind a single endpoint, loading them on demand and sharing resources to reduce cost.

options.text: One dedicated real-time endpoint per model.
options.explanation: Hundreds of separate endpoints multiply cost and management overhead.

options.text: A batch transform job per model.
options.explanation: Batch transform is for offline scoring, not on-demand hosting of many models.

options.text: Serverless inference with one function per model and no sharing.
options.explanation: This does not provide the on-demand shared hosting that multi-model endpoints are designed for.

questions.question: A team deploys several large language models to GPU instances and wants to pack multiple models and copies onto shared accelerators, scale each independently, and scale unused ones to zero to cut cost. Which capability supports this?

options.text: SageMaker inference components.
options.explanation: Inference components let you deploy multiple models/copies on shared instances, allocate resources per component, scale them independently, and scale to zero.

options.text: A single-model real-time endpoint per model only.
options.explanation: Single-model endpoints cannot pack multiple models onto shared accelerators with independent scaling.

options.text: Batch transform.
options.explanation: Batch transform is offline scoring, not GPU packing of online models.

options.text: Ground Truth.
options.explanation: Ground Truth labels data and is unrelated to serving.

questions.question: Traffic to a real-time endpoint varies widely between business hours and nights. The team wants the endpoint to add and remove instances automatically based on load. What should they configure?

options.text: Automatic scaling (for example, target-tracking on invocations per instance) for the endpoint.
options.explanation: SageMaker endpoint auto scaling adjusts instance count based on a target metric such as invocations per instance, matching capacity to demand.

options.text: A fixed, manually chosen instance count sized for peak.
options.explanation: A fixed peak size wastes money at night and cannot adapt automatically.

options.text: Switch to batch transform during the day.
options.explanation: Batch transform is offline and cannot serve real-time daytime traffic.

options.text: Raise the model temperature during peak hours.
options.explanation: Temperature does not affect endpoint capacity.

questions.question: Before promoting a new model version, a team wants to send a copy of live production traffic to it and compare results without affecting users. Which SageMaker capability provides this?

options.text: Shadow testing (shadow variants).
options.explanation: Shadow testing mirrors live traffic to a shadow variant so you can validate a new version against real requests without impacting production responses.

options.text: Batch transform on yesterday's data.
options.explanation: Offline batch scoring does not exercise the new version against live production traffic.

options.text: Deleting the old endpoint first.
options.explanation: Removing the current endpoint would disrupt users and provides no safe comparison.

options.text: Increasing max tokens on the new version.
options.explanation: Output length settings are unrelated to safely comparing versions on live traffic.

questions.question: A developer wants to quickly deploy a popular open foundation model with minimal setup, including a tested container and example notebooks, to get an endpoint running fast. Which option is best?

options.text: Deploy the model from Amazon SageMaker JumpStart.
options.explanation: JumpStart offers prebuilt, deployable foundation and task models with curated containers and example notebooks for fast, low-effort deployment.

options.text: Build a custom container from scratch and write all serving code.
options.explanation: Building everything yourself is the slowest, highest-effort path.

options.text: Use batch transform to expose an endpoint.
options.explanation: Batch transform does not create an online endpoint.

options.text: Use Amazon Comprehend.
options.explanation: Comprehend is a managed NLP service, not a way to deploy an arbitrary foundation model.

questions.question: A team must score a large, fixed dataset that already sits in Amazon S3 in a single offline run, with no need for a persistent endpoint. Which SageMaker option is MOST appropriate and cost-effective?

options.text: A batch transform job.
options.explanation: Batch transform processes a whole dataset from S3 in one offline run and tears down compute afterward, with no persistent endpoint to pay for.

options.text: A real-time endpoint invoked in a loop.
options.explanation: Looping requests over a persistent endpoint is more expensive and complex than a single batch job.

options.text: A serverless endpoint with manual paging.
options.explanation: Serverless endpoints target intermittent online requests, not bulk offline scoring of a fixed dataset.

options.text: An asynchronous endpoint for each record.
options.explanation: Per-record async calls add unnecessary overhead versus one batch transform over the dataset.

questions.question: When updating a production endpoint to a new model, a team wants a controlled rollout that shifts a small percentage of traffic first and automatically rolls back if errors spike. Which SageMaker feature provides this?

options.text: Deployment guardrails with a canary (or linear) traffic-shifting strategy and automatic rollback.
options.explanation: SageMaker deployment guardrails shift traffic gradually (canary/linear), monitor alarms, and roll back automatically if the new version misbehaves.

options.text: Deleting and recreating the endpoint in place.
options.explanation: An in-place replace gives no gradual shift or automatic rollback and risks downtime.

options.text: Batch transform validation only.
options.explanation: Offline batch validation does not control live traffic shifting or rollback.

options.text: Raising the instance count during the switch.
options.explanation: More instances does not provide controlled traffic shifting or automatic rollback.

questions.question: Developers want inline, context-aware code suggestions and the ability to generate functions from natural-language comments directly inside their IDE to speed up routine coding. Which capability provides this?

options.text: Amazon Q Developer in-IDE code suggestions and generation.
options.explanation: Amazon Q Developer integrates with the IDE to provide real-time inline completions and generate code from natural-language prompts.

options.text: Amazon QuickSight Q.
options.explanation: QuickSight Q answers natural-language questions about BI data, not IDE code generation.

options.text: Amazon Comprehend.
options.explanation: Comprehend is an NLP analysis service, not a coding assistant.

options.text: Amazon Kendra.
options.explanation: Kendra is enterprise search, not an IDE coding assistant.

questions.question: A team wants an assistant that can take a natural-language feature request, plan the change, and implement it across multiple files in their project. Which Amazon Q Developer capability is designed for this?

options.text: The Amazon Q Developer feature-development agent (/dev), which plans and applies multi-file changes.
options.explanation: The Q Developer agent for software development analyzes the codebase, proposes a plan, and generates coordinated changes across multiple files.

options.text: Single-line autocomplete only.
options.explanation: Basic autocomplete cannot plan and implement a multi-file feature.

options.text: A CloudWatch dashboard.
options.explanation: Dashboards visualize metrics; they do not implement code features.

options.text: Increasing the model temperature.
options.explanation: Temperature does not provide multi-file feature implementation.

questions.question: An enterprise must upgrade many applications from an old Java version to a current LTS release and wants to automate the repetitive code changes at scale. Which Amazon Q Developer capability helps MOST?

options.text: Amazon Q Developer code transformation (automated language/framework upgrades).
options.explanation: Q Developer's transformation capability automates large-scale upgrades, such as moving codebases to a newer Java version, reducing manual effort.

options.text: Manually editing each file by hand.
options.explanation: Manual edits across many applications are slow and error-prone, which automation avoids.

options.text: Amazon Macie.
options.explanation: Macie classifies sensitive data and has nothing to do with code upgrades.

options.text: Amazon Bedrock Guardrails.
options.explanation: Guardrails govern model output content, not code transformations.

questions.question: A security-conscious team wants their assistant to scan code for vulnerabilities and suggest remediations as part of development. Which Amazon Q Developer capability provides this?

options.text: Amazon Q Developer security scanning, which detects vulnerabilities and suggests fixes.
options.explanation: Q Developer can scan code for security issues and recommend remediations directly in the developer workflow.

options.text: Amazon GuardDuty agent on the laptop.
options.explanation: GuardDuty monitors AWS accounts and workloads for threats, not in-IDE source code scanning for developers.

options.text: Raising the temperature parameter.
options.explanation: Temperature is unrelated to code security scanning.

options.text: Amazon Athena queries.
options.explanation: Athena queries data in S3 and does not scan code for vulnerabilities.

questions.question: To improve coverage and velocity, a team wants to automatically generate unit tests for existing functions. Which capability supports this with the least manual effort?

options.text: Amazon Q Developer unit test generation.
options.explanation: Q Developer can generate unit tests for existing code, increasing coverage with minimal manual effort.

options.text: Writing every test by hand with no tooling.
options.explanation: Hand-writing all tests is the slowest option and is what the team wants to avoid.

options.text: Amazon QuickSight.
options.explanation: QuickSight is for dashboards, not test generation.

options.text: Amazon Comprehend.
options.explanation: Comprehend performs NLP analysis, not unit test generation.

questions.question: A team must retain the full request and response content of every Amazon Bedrock model call for troubleshooting and audit. Which capability captures this with the LEAST custom code?

options.text: Enable Amazon Bedrock model invocation logging to Amazon S3 (and/or CloudWatch Logs).
options.explanation: Model invocation logging is a built-in feature that records full prompts, responses, and metadata to S3 and/or CloudWatch with no custom instrumentation.

options.text: Add print statements in the application and hope they are captured.
options.explanation: Ad hoc logging is incomplete and unreliable compared with built-in invocation logging.

options.text: Enable AWS CloudTrail only.
options.explanation: CloudTrail records API activity metadata, not the full prompt and response content.

options.text: Use Amazon Macie on the application logs.
options.explanation: Macie classifies sensitive data; it does not capture model request/response content.

questions.question: A finance team needs to attribute Amazon Bedrock usage and cost to specific applications and teams that share the same models. Which approach is purpose-built for this?

options.text: Use Amazon Bedrock application inference profiles with cost allocation tags to track usage and cost per application.
options.explanation: Application inference profiles let you route and tag invocations so usage and cost can be tracked and attributed per application or team.

options.text: Guess each team's share from total spend.
options.explanation: Estimation without tagging is inaccurate and not auditable.

options.text: Raise the temperature to reduce cost.
options.explanation: Temperature does not attribute or reduce cost.

options.text: Disable logging to lower the bill.
options.explanation: Disabling logging removes visibility and does not attribute cost.

questions.question: An application intermittently receives throttling errors from Amazon Bedrock, and operators want to be alerted automatically when throttling rises. What should they configure?

options.text: A CloudWatch alarm on the Bedrock throttling metric for the model.
options.explanation: Bedrock publishes CloudWatch metrics (including throttling), so an alarm on the throttle metric notifies operators when throttling increases.

options.text: Nothing; throttling cannot be monitored.
options.explanation: Throttling is observable through CloudWatch metrics and can be alarmed on.

options.text: Increase max tokens to avoid throttling.
options.explanation: Output length does not address request-rate throttling or provide alerting.

options.text: Lower the temperature to reduce throttling.
options.explanation: Temperature has no effect on throttling or its monitoring.

questions.question: A generative AI request flows through API Gateway, several Lambda functions, a knowledge base, and Bedrock, and the team needs to find which component causes high end-to-end latency. Which service provides distributed tracing to localize the bottleneck?

options.text: AWS X-Ray.
options.explanation: X-Ray traces requests across distributed components, producing a service map and segment timings that pinpoint the slow part of the chain.

options.text: Amazon Macie.
options.explanation: Macie classifies sensitive data and does not trace request latency.

options.text: Amazon Comprehend.
options.explanation: Comprehend analyzes text and is not a tracing tool.

options.text: AWS Glue.
options.explanation: Glue is for ETL, not distributed tracing.

questions.question: Invocation logs are being written to CloudWatch Logs, and an engineer needs to interactively query them to find error patterns and aggregate failures. Which tool should they use?

options.text: Amazon CloudWatch Logs Insights.
options.explanation: Logs Insights provides an interactive query language to search, filter, and aggregate log data such as invocation logs to surface error patterns.

options.text: Amazon QuickSight Q on raw log files.
options.explanation: QuickSight Q answers questions over prepared BI datasets, not ad hoc CloudWatch log querying.

options.text: Amazon Rekognition.
options.explanation: Rekognition analyzes images and video, not logs.

options.text: Increasing the log retention period only.
options.explanation: Longer retention stores logs but does not provide query and aggregation capabilities.

questions.question: A model deployed on a SageMaker real-time endpoint must be monitored for data drift and degradation in input/prediction quality over time, with automated alerts. Which capability is designed for this?

options.text: Amazon SageMaker Model Monitor.
options.explanation: Model Monitor continuously checks deployed endpoints for data quality, drift, and other issues against a baseline and can raise alerts.

options.text: AWS CloudTrail.
options.explanation: CloudTrail records API calls, not model data drift.

options.text: Amazon Bedrock Guardrails.
options.explanation: Guardrails filter content; they do not monitor endpoint data drift.

options.text: Amazon Athena.
options.explanation: Athena is a query service and does not monitor model drift.

questions.question: After launch, a team wants to continuously assure the quality of a generative feature in production, catching regressions as prompts and models change. Which practice is MOST effective?

options.text: Log responses and run periodic automated evaluations on sampled production traffic, alerting on quality regressions.
options.explanation: Combining response logging with scheduled automated evaluation on samples provides ongoing, data-driven quality monitoring and early regression detection.

options.text: Only watch CPU and memory metrics.
options.explanation: Infrastructure metrics do not reveal degradations in generation quality.

options.text: Wait for users to report problems.
options.explanation: Relying on complaints is reactive and lets defects reach users.

options.text: Run a single pre-launch test and never re-check.
options.explanation: A one-time test misses regressions introduced by later changes.

questions.question: A mobile app must let many end users call Amazon Bedrock without embedding AWS credentials in the app. The team wants each user to receive scoped, temporary AWS credentials after signing in. Which approach fits best?

options.text: Use Amazon Cognito identity pools to exchange a sign-in for temporary, scoped AWS credentials.
options.explanation: Cognito identity pools federate authenticated users to temporary AWS credentials tied to an IAM role, avoiding embedded long-lived keys.

options.text: Embed a shared IAM access key in the mobile app.
options.explanation: Embedding long-lived keys in a distributed app is a serious security risk.

options.text: Give every user the root account credentials.
options.explanation: Sharing root credentials is never acceptable and grossly violates least privilege.

options.text: Store one IAM user's secret in the app config for everyone.
options.explanation: A shared long-lived secret cannot be scoped per user and is easily compromised.

questions.question: A regulated company must call Amazon Bedrock from workloads in its VPC without traversing the public internet. Which solution provides private connectivity?

options.text: Create VPC interface endpoints (AWS PrivateLink) for Amazon Bedrock.
options.explanation: Interface VPC endpoints via PrivateLink keep traffic to Bedrock on the AWS network, avoiding the public internet.

options.text: Route all Bedrock calls through a public NAT gateway only.
options.explanation: A NAT gateway still sends traffic over the public internet, not a private path.

options.text: Disable encryption to simplify networking.
options.explanation: Disabling encryption weakens security and does not provide private connectivity.

options.text: Use the model temperature to control routing.
options.explanation: Temperature is unrelated to network connectivity.

questions.question: A company must encrypt its Bedrock custom model artifacts and knowledge base data at rest using keys it controls and can audit and rotate. Which approach should it use?

options.text: Encrypt with an AWS KMS customer managed key (CMK).
options.explanation: A customer managed KMS key gives the company control over key policies, rotation, and CloudTrail auditing of key usage for data at rest.

options.text: Rely on no encryption to reduce latency.
options.explanation: Operating without encryption fails the data-protection requirement.

options.text: Store the data in a public S3 bucket.
options.explanation: Public exposure is the opposite of the security requirement.

options.text: Hard-code an encryption passphrase in the app.
options.explanation: A hard-coded passphrase is insecure and is not a managed, auditable key.

questions.question: An application in Account A must call Amazon Bedrock resources governed by Account B, using temporary credentials and no long-lived keys. Which mechanism is appropriate?

options.text: Create an IAM role in Account B and have Account A assume it with AWS STS AssumeRole.
options.explanation: Cross-account IAM roles assumed via STS provide temporary credentials and scoped permissions without sharing long-lived keys.

options.text: Email an IAM access key from Account B to Account A.
options.explanation: Sharing long-lived access keys is insecure and unmanageable.

options.text: Make all Bedrock resources public.
options.explanation: Public access removes the access control the requirement depends on.

options.text: Use the same root user for both accounts.
options.explanation: Sharing root credentials across accounts is a severe security violation.

questions.question: An agent's AWS Lambda function must call a third-party API that requires a secret API key, which must be stored securely and rotated without code changes. Which service should hold the key?

options.text: AWS Secrets Manager.
options.explanation: Secrets Manager securely stores credentials, controls access via IAM, and supports rotation without changing application code.

options.text: A plaintext environment variable committed to source control.
options.explanation: Committing secrets to source control exposes them and provides no rotation.

options.text: A hard-coded constant in the Lambda code.
options.explanation: Hard-coded secrets cannot be rotated safely and risk exposure.

options.text: An unencrypted object in a public S3 bucket.
options.explanation: Public, unencrypted storage of a secret is a serious vulnerability.

questions.question: Security policy requires that a given IAM role be allowed to invoke only one specific approved foundation model in Amazon Bedrock and no others. How is this BEST enforced?

options.text: Write a least-privilege IAM policy that allows invoke actions only on the approved model's resource ARN.
options.explanation: Scoping the IAM policy to the specific model ARN enforces that the role can invoke only the approved model, following least privilege.

options.text: Allow all Bedrock models and remind developers to use only the approved one.
options.explanation: A broad allow with a verbal reminder does not technically restrict access.

options.text: Lower the temperature on the disallowed models.
options.explanation: Temperature does not restrict which models a role may call.

options.text: Rely on the application code to choose the right model.
options.explanation: Application-side selection is not an access control and can be bypassed.

questions.question: A compliance team must ensure that customer prompts are processed only within an approved AWS Region and are not used to train the base foundation models. Which statement and configuration are correct?

options.text: Use Amazon Bedrock in the approved Region; Bedrock processes data in that Region and does not use your prompts or outputs to train the base models.
options.explanation: Bedrock keeps inference data within the Region you call and does not use customer inputs/outputs to train base models, so selecting the approved Region meets the requirement.

options.text: You must file a support ticket or it will train on your data by default.
options.explanation: Bedrock does not use your inputs/outputs to train base models, so no opt-out ticket is required for that.

options.text: Data is always processed globally regardless of Region.
options.explanation: Bedrock processes your inference data in the Region where you invoke the model, not globally.

options.text: Only running your own self-hosted model can prevent base-model training on your data.
options.explanation: Using Bedrock already prevents your data from training the base models; self-hosting is not required for that guarantee.

questions.question: A team needs to filter harmful content (hate, insults, violence, sexual content, and misconduct) in both prompts and responses, with adjustable strictness per category. Which Amazon Bedrock Guardrails feature provides this?

options.text: Content filters with configurable strength thresholds per harmful category.
options.explanation: Guardrails content filters detect and block harmful categories in input and output, with adjustable filter strengths per category.

options.text: Stop sequences.
options.explanation: Stop sequences end generation at a phrase; they do not classify or block harmful categories.

options.text: Lowering the temperature.
options.explanation: Temperature does not filter harmful content.

options.text: Provisioned Throughput.
options.explanation: Provisioned Throughput is a capacity feature, unrelated to content filtering.

questions.question: A banking assistant must never discuss a specific prohibited subject (for example, giving individualized investment advice), even when users phrase the request in new ways. Which Guardrails feature is designed for this?

options.text: Denied topics, defined with a description and examples so the guardrail blocks that subject.
options.explanation: Denied topics let you define subjects to block by description and examples, so the guardrail refuses related requests regardless of phrasing.

options.text: Increasing max tokens.
options.explanation: Output length does not block a subject.

options.text: A higher temperature.
options.explanation: Temperature does not enforce topic restrictions.

options.text: A larger context window.
options.explanation: Context size does not prevent a prohibited topic from being discussed.

questions.question: An assistant must ensure that any personally identifiable information appearing in model responses is masked or blocked before reaching users. Which Guardrails capability addresses this?

options.text: The sensitive information (PII) filter, which can mask or block PII in inputs and outputs.
options.explanation: Guardrails can detect PII and either mask it (for example, redact) or block the response, protecting users from exposure.

options.text: Stop sequences on names.
options.explanation: Stop sequences cannot reliably detect and redact varied PII entities.

options.text: Lowering the temperature.
options.explanation: Temperature does not detect or mask PII.

options.text: A custom font in the UI.
options.explanation: Presentation styling does not remove PII from the content.

questions.question: A public chatbot is targeted by users trying to override its instructions and extract restricted behavior. Which Amazon Bedrock Guardrails capability specifically helps detect these attempts?

options.text: The prompt attack filter, which detects prompt injection and jailbreak attempts.
options.explanation: Guardrails include a prompt attack filter that flags prompt injection and jailbreak inputs so they can be blocked.

options.text: Increasing the temperature to confuse attackers.
options.explanation: Temperature does not detect or stop prompt attacks.

options.text: Adding more few-shot examples of jokes.
options.explanation: Unrelated examples do not provide injection detection.

options.text: Switching to batch inference.
options.explanation: Batch inference is a serving mode and does not detect prompt attacks.

questions.question: A company runs a self-managed RAG pipeline and even calls a model hosted outside Amazon Bedrock, but it wants to apply the same Bedrock guardrail policies to arbitrary text at chosen points in the flow. Which capability enables this?

options.text: The ApplyGuardrail API, which evaluates text against a guardrail independently of a model invocation.
options.explanation: ApplyGuardrail lets you assess input or output text against a guardrail at any point, including content from models not hosted in Bedrock.

options.text: Only inline guardrails on Bedrock InvokeModel.
options.explanation: Inline-only guardrails would not cover text from external models or arbitrary pipeline points, which ApplyGuardrail handles.

options.text: Raising the temperature.
options.explanation: Temperature is unrelated to applying guardrail policies.

options.text: Provisioned Throughput.
options.explanation: Provisioned Throughput is a capacity feature, not a guardrail evaluation API.

questions.question: In a RAG assistant, the team wants the guardrail itself to block responses that are not supported by the retrieved source or that stray from the user's query, using a tunable threshold. Which Guardrails capability fits?

options.text: The contextual grounding and relevance checks, with configurable thresholds.
options.explanation: Guardrails contextual grounding and relevance checks score whether a response is grounded in the source and relevant to the query, and can block low-scoring responses based on thresholds you set.

options.text: Denied topics only.
options.explanation: Denied topics block subjects but do not measure grounding in the retrieved source.

options.text: Stop sequences.
options.explanation: Stop sequences end generation and cannot assess grounding.

options.text: A larger model temperature.
options.explanation: Temperature does not assess or enforce grounding.

questions.question: A regulated insurer needs strong assurance that policy-related answers conform to its written rules, with verifiable checks rather than only probabilistic filtering. Which newer Amazon Bedrock Guardrails capability is designed to mathematically verify outputs against encoded policies?

options.text: Automated Reasoning checks.
options.explanation: Automated Reasoning checks use formal, logic-based verification against an encoded policy to validate whether responses are consistent with the rules, reducing factual errors in regulated domains.

options.text: Increasing the temperature.
options.explanation: Temperature does not verify outputs against policy.

options.text: Stop sequences.
options.explanation: Stop sequences end generation; they do not verify policy conformance.

options.text: Provisioned Throughput.
options.explanation: Provisioned Throughput is a capacity feature, not a verification capability.

questions.question: A financial firm must store generated compliance records so they cannot be altered or deleted for a mandated retention period, even by administrators. Which configuration meets this requirement?

options.text: Store records in Amazon S3 with Object Lock in compliance mode for the required retention period.
options.explanation: S3 Object Lock compliance mode enforces write-once-read-many (WORM) immutability so objects cannot be modified or deleted, even by the root user, until retention expires.

options.text: Store records in a standard S3 bucket and ask staff not to delete them.
options.explanation: A policy request does not technically prevent modification or deletion.

options.text: Keep records only in application memory.
options.explanation: In-memory storage is volatile and provides no durable, immutable retention.

options.text: Use S3 versioning alone without Object Lock.
options.explanation: Versioning preserves prior versions but does not prevent deletion or enforce a compliance retention lock.

questions.question: An auditor must determine which IAM principals called which Amazon Bedrock APIs, and when, across the account. Which service provides this audit trail?

options.text: AWS CloudTrail.
options.explanation: CloudTrail records API calls including the principal, action, time, and source, providing the management/audit trail of who did what.

options.text: Amazon CloudWatch dashboards only.
options.explanation: Dashboards visualize metrics but do not provide the per-call identity and action audit trail.

options.text: Amazon Macie.
options.explanation: Macie classifies sensitive data; it does not record API caller identity and actions.

options.text: Amazon Comprehend.
options.explanation: Comprehend analyzes text and is not an API audit service.

questions.question: For regulatory reconstruction, a company must retain the actual prompt content and model responses (not just API metadata) for every Bedrock call, in immutable storage. Which combination meets this?

options.text: Enable Amazon Bedrock model invocation logging to Amazon S3 and apply S3 Object Lock for the retention period.
options.explanation: Invocation logging captures full prompt and response content, and Object Lock makes the stored logs immutable for the mandated retention.

options.text: Enable AWS CloudTrail alone.
options.explanation: CloudTrail captures API metadata, not the full prompt and response content required for reconstruction.

options.text: Print prompts to the application console only.
options.explanation: Console output is not durable, complete, or immutable for compliance.

options.text: Rely on CloudWatch metrics for content.
options.explanation: Metrics are numeric aggregates and do not store prompt or response text.

questions.question: A company wants to keep customer chat transcripts for analytics but must comply with privacy rules that prohibit retaining personally identifiable information. What is the BEST approach before storing the transcripts?

options.text: Redact PII from the transcripts with Amazon Comprehend before storing the sanitized text.
options.explanation: Detecting and redacting PII with Comprehend before storage lets the company keep useful analytics data while complying with privacy requirements.

options.text: Store the raw transcripts unchanged for completeness.
options.explanation: Storing raw PII violates the privacy requirement.

options.text: Raise the model temperature to obscure PII.
options.explanation: Temperature does not remove PII from stored text.

options.text: Encrypt the bucket and keep all PII indefinitely.
options.explanation: Encryption protects data at rest but does not satisfy a rule against retaining PII at all.

questions.question: Stored embeddings and invocation logs contain sensitive business data, and the security team must control and audit the encryption keys used to protect them at rest. Which approach should they adopt?

options.text: Encrypt the data with AWS KMS customer managed keys and restrict and audit key usage via key policies and CloudTrail.
options.explanation: Customer managed KMS keys give the team control over rotation and access, and CloudTrail records key usage for auditing.

options.text: Leave the data unencrypted to simplify access.
options.explanation: Unencrypted sensitive data fails the data-protection requirement.

options.text: Use a single shared password stored in code.
options.explanation: A hard-coded shared secret is insecure and not an auditable managed key.

options.text: Make the storage public so keys are unnecessary.
options.explanation: Public storage exposes the sensitive data and is the opposite of the requirement.
